From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
Cc: Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>, Tim Cho <tim.cho@odinlabs.ai>, Eleanor Hayes <eleanor.hayes@odinlabs.ai>
Date: Thu, 12 Aug 2027 08:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Odin Labs — Dedicated GPU pool turn-up, proposed schedule and constraints

Ben / team / Odin —

Thanks for the details. I drafted a lean ops checklist and a cutover run sequence below. I also attached a small CSV (netops-access-template.csv) for the allowlist exchange.

Ops checklist (short):
- NetOps exchange: collect VPC CIDRs, static NATs, and peering contact (use attached CSV).
- Access: temporary bastion + least-privilege IAM roles for deployment window (we provide time-limited tokens).
- Model artifacts: confirm model tarball + exact HF-compatible config and tokenizer versions.
- Autoscale profile: start with conservative scale thresholds (CPU < 65% && queue depth > 30) and aggressive burst policy (instant add up to 2x nodes with warm-cache handoff).
- KV/prefix caching: enable sticky KV for streaming sessions; handoff plan included to minimize replays.
- Observability: enable token-level tracing for first 72 hours; we’ll keep detailed logs for 30 days (we can extend per request).
- KMS/HSM: we support customer-managed KMS and HSM-backed envelopes. Naomi, can you produce the short compliance note requested by Odin?

Cutover run sequence (staging -> canary -> full):
1) Staging turn-up: provision pool in us-west-2 with routing to a staging gateway. Run model smoke tests.
2) Canary: route 5% traffic for 6 hours, validate stream preservation during node additions.
3) Burst test: simulate 2x burst (we can run synthetic load using the odn-autoscale-sim.csv).
4) Full cutover: increase to 100% if canary passes checks (latency, token success rate, no dropped streams).

Notes on streaming + burst: our runtime will attempt a warm-cache handoff for new nodes; in-flight streams are preserved by proxying tokens during rekeying where possible. There is a small window (~3–8s) of elevated latency while caches converge; we’ll instrument and roll metrics to you.

Naomi: can you attach the KMS/HSM note and the suggested retention window? Karthik: initial capacity estimate please (vGPU counts + memory) by EOD tomorrow.

Attachments: netops-access-template.csv

—
Monica Patel
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
monica.patel@redwood.com



From: Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Tim Cho <tim.cho@odinlabs.ai>, Eleanor Hayes <eleanor.hayes@odinlabs.ai>
Date: Fri, 13 Aug 2027 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Odin Labs — Dedicated GPU pool turn-up, proposed schedule and constraints

Ben / all —

Attached short note on KMS/HSM and audit retention (inline summary below). We support:
- Customer-managed KMS: AES-256 envelope encryption with customer key; logging of key usage to CloudWatch + exported audit logs.
- HSM: available on private deployments; for Hosted/Dedicated we provide HSM-backed envelopes via partner HSM if required (adds ~5 business days to onboarding).
- Audit retention: default 30 days for token-level traces; recommended 90 days for compliance customers. We can export logs to customer S3 (recommended).

Capacity quick estimate (ballpark):
- Baseline 120 RPS sustained, median payload: 1.4KB -> estimate 6 x A100-80GB equiv vGPUs for baseline (multi-model routing overhead included).
- Peak 1,100 concurrent streaming -> requires additional 10 vGPUs to stay within latency SLOs.
- With 2x burst margin we recommend reserving 32 vGPUs in the pool and enabling autoscale to add up to that on demand; actual placement depends on model memory.

I’ll attach the full spreadsheet (odn-autoscale-sim.csv) to HubSpot and to the shared Drive link. If Odin needs stricter retention or HSM-first policy, expect an additional procurement/ops step.

—
Naomi Feldman
Head of Implementation, Redwood Inference
naomi.feldman@redwood.com



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Eleanor Hayes <eleanor.hayes@odinlabs.ai>, Tim Cho <tim.cho@odinlabs.ai>, procurement@odinlabs.ai
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Naomi Feldman <naomi.feldman@redwood.com>, Aditya Rao <aditya.rao@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Aug 2027 16:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Odin Labs — Dedicated GPU pool turn-up, proposed schedule and constraints — confirmed kickoff and next steps

Eleanor / Tim / Procurement —

Thanks everyone for the quick turnaround. We’re aligned on region (us-west-2 primary, eu-west-1 fallback), and the plan below represents agreed next steps:

1) NetOps exchange: Tim/Eleanor to return filled netops-access-template.csv by Tue 17 Aug EOD.
2) Naomi & Karthik: finalize capacity reservation (reserve 32 vGPUs) and post the final numbers to HubSpot by Tue 17 Aug EOD.
3) Monica: send Ops runbook + ephemeral access tokens for the Aug 20–22 staging window.
4) Ben: schedule 30-min kickoff with Odin NetOps + Redwood SRE for Aug 18 at 9am PT (invite sent).
5) Procurement: Ben will share the DocuSign SOW once Odin confirms peering vs static NAT preference.

If we can get the netops CSV and capacity numbers by Tuesday we’ll start staging provisioning on Aug 19 and run the canary on Aug 24 as discussed. We will also enable extended tracing for the first 72 hours and keep token-level logs for 30 days (can extend to 90 on request).

Attachments: Odin-Dedicated-SOW-draft.pdf (for DocuSign prep)

Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

+1 (415) 555-0147

- HSM: available on private deployments; for Hosted/Dedicated we provide HSM-backed envelopes via partner HSM if required (adds ~5 business days to onboarding).
- Audit retention: default 30 days for token-level traces; recommended 90 days for compliance customers. We can export logs to customer S3 (recommended).

Capacity quick estimate (ballpark):
- Baseline 120 RPS sustained, median payload: 1.4KB -> estimate 6 x A100-80GB equiv vGPUs for baseline (multi-model routing overhead included).
- Peak 1,100 concurrent streaming -> requires additional 10 vGPUs to stay within latency SLOs.
- With 2x burst margin we recommend reserving 32 vGPUs in the pool and enabling autoscale to add up to that on demand; actual placement depends on model memory.

I’ll attach the full spreadsheet (odn-autoscale-sim.csv) to HubSpot and to the shared Drive link. If Odin needs stricter retention or HSM-first policy, expect an additional procurement/ops step.

—
Naomi Feldman
Head of Implementation, Redwood Inference
naomi.feldman@redwood.com

Date: Sun, 15 Aug 2027 16:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Odin Labs — Dedicated GPU pool turn-up, proposed schedule and constraints — confirmed kickoff and next steps

Eleanor / Tim / Procurement —

Thanks everyone for the quick turnaround. We’re aligned on region (us-west-2 primary, eu-west-1 fallback), and the plan below represents agreed next steps:

1) NetOps exchange: Tim/Eleanor to return filled netops-access-template.csv by Tue 17 Aug EOD.
2) Naomi & Karthik: finalize capacity reservation (reserve 32 vGPUs) and post the final numbers to HubSpot by Tue 17 Aug EOD.
3) Monica: send Ops runbook + ephemeral access tokens for the Aug 20–22 staging window.
4) Ben: schedule 30-min kickoff with Odin NetOps + Redwood SRE for Aug 18 at 9am PT (invite sent).
5) Procurement: Ben will share the DocuSign SOW once Odin confirms peering vs static NAT preference.

If we can get the netops CSV and capacity numbers by Tuesday we’ll start staging provisioning on Aug 19 and run the canary on Aug 24 as discussed. We will also enable extended tracing for the first 72 hours and keep token-level logs for 30 days (can extend to 90 on request).

Attachments: Odin-Dedicated-SOW-draft.pdf (for DocuSign prep)

Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

title: Customer P1: sudden latency + 429s during promotional surge — requires exec approval
preview:
From: Kai Nguyen <kai@fastretail.co>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: accounts@fastretail.co
Date: Sun, 15 Aug 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Urgent – Checkout failures and timeouts during flash sale

Ben —

We started a planned flash sale at 08:30 PT and within 20 minutes we saw a sharp rise in checkout latency and a growing number of 429 / rate-limit responses coming back from the Redwood API. This is impacting real purchases; our payments vendor has queued multiple transactions.

Numbers from our monitoring: 95th latency jumped from ~120ms to >2.5s, and 429s spiked to ~18% of token requests for the first hour. We also saw throughput drop despite the surge — requests appear to be throttled.

This is P1 for us. Please escalate to engineering and ops immediately. We need a mitigation plan and to understand whether this is a quota/burst setting or a service-side degradation.

Attached: quick capture of request logs and simple error sample.

— Kai
Kai Nguyen
CTO, FastRetail Inc.
kai@fastretail.co


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Aug 2027 09:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Urgent – Checkout failures and timeouts during flash sale

Forwarding Kai's note — P1 for FastRetail (enterprise customer, committed capacity). Marcus/Evelyn: need immediate triage. Key asks:
- Confirm whether we're hitting per-tenant rate/quota or a global throttling event.
- If quota, can we temporarily raise FastRetail's burst window + overall TPS for the next 2 hours?
- If it's a config bug, rollback or safe fallback to compatible model variant.

Customer is past us on urgency. Vivek, looping you since this looks like a capacity/policy decision that may need exec sign-off for emergency bump and credits.

Ben
--
Ben Carter
Account Executive — Enterprise
Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Cc: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Aug 2027 09:48:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Urgent – Checkout failures and timeouts during flash sale

Ben / team — quick summary from the runtime side after pulling logs and policy configs (attached fastretail_logs_0815.zip, rate_limit_config_snapshot.png):

Observations:
- FastRetail is on Dedicated with a reserved pool, but we recently auto-applied a safety quota change: per-tenant rolling burst window was reduced from 120s to 30s in the new quota profile (change merged 2027-08-10) to protect multi-tenant hosted capacity.
- During the flash sale the client's request pattern had short high-frequency bursts that exceed the new 30s burst allowance, so the policy started returning 429s with a short Retry-After and also triggered continuous KV-cache misses which increased latency.
- Kernel/hardware utilization across the cluster is healthy; not a global GPU shortage. This is policy configuration acting as an unintended hard throttle.

Immediate actions I recommend (can execute now):
1) Increase FastRetail's burst window back to 120s and raise their per-second TPS limit by 3x for the next 3 hours. This is a config flip only and does not require node changes.
2) Enable canary fallback to the slightly higher-latency but less cost-efficient variant so we can route overflow traffic without 429s.
3) Send customer a short mitigation note and ask them to hold additional marketing pushes for the next 2 hours while we observe.

If approved I can flip (1) and (2) in ~6 minutes and then monitor the reduction in 429s.

— Marcus
Marcus Lin
Senior SRE, Serving Runtime
Redwood Inference


From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
To: Kai Nguyen <kai@fastretail.co>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Aug 2027 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Urgent – Checkout failures and timeouts during flash sale

Kai —

Thank you for the quick report and the logs. We're on this as a P1. Marcus has identified that a recent quota profile change reduced your burst window which is causing the throttles during the short spikes in traffic.

Immediate mitigation starting now:
- We're increasing your burst window and TPS for the next 3 hours, will confirm when complete (Marcus is executing).
- We're routing any overflow to the fallback model variant to reduce 429s while we observe.

We estimate this will materially reduce checkout failures within 10–15 minutes. I'll follow up with an RCA and next steps for permanent policy adjustments and credits. 

Apologies for the impact — we know this is critical for today's promotion.

Best,
Evelyn Hart
Customer Success Manager
Redwood Inference


From: Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 15 Aug 2027 15:38:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Urgent – Checkout failures and timeouts during flash sale

Thanks team — good triage. Marcus, proceed with your recommended toggles now and keep an eye on tail latency and 429s (confirm reduction).

Operational decisions:
- Approve emergency burst window increase and TPS bump for 6 hours (extra guardrails: limit total token spend to projected surge + 20%).
- Approve 15% credit against today's overage (customer-facing rollback to be negotiated by Ben/Laura).

Longer term: schedule a meeting with Product/Runtime to revisit burst-window defaults and a safer rollout process for quota profile changes. Marcus/Laura, please prepare a short write-up for Tuesday's ops sync including rollback playbook.

Vivek
Vivek Kulkarni
VP, Revenue & Customer Ops
Redwood Inference

title: Contract renewal: Move invoicing to Azure Marketplace (SkyBridge / Redwood)
preview:
From: Timothy Hale <tim.hale@skybridge.gov>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@skybridge.gov>
Date: 2027-08-11T09:02:00-04:00
Subject: Contract Renewal — billing via Azure Marketplace (SkyBridge / Redwood)

Hi Ava,

We have an upcoming renewal for our contract (SKY-1819) with Redwood that needs to align with SkyBridge procurement rules. For FY28 we are required to route software purchases through Azure Marketplace (for audit and internal PO flow).

Can Redwood bill via Azure Marketplace for the renewal effective 2027-09-15? If so, what do you need from our side (PO, procurement contact, signer) and what is the timeline to appear as an Azure Marketplace subscription to our finance team?

We'd also like to understand whether the renewal can carry any existing committed discounts and whether there will be an invoice example we can share with our accounts payable.

Thanks — please loop in whoever at Redwood owns the marketplace onboarding.

Timothy Hale
Head of Procurement, SkyBridge Systems
Tim.Hale@skybridge.gov
Office: +1-202-555-0126

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Timothy Hale <tim.hale@skybridge.gov>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-08-11T10:18:00-04:00
Subject: Re: Contract Renewal — billing via Azure Marketplace (SkyBridge / Redwood)

Hi Timothy —

Thanks for the heads up and the clear timing. Short answer: yes, we can convert the renewal to Azure Marketplace invoicing, and we can preserve the commercial terms in most cases, but there are a couple of items we need to coordinate:

- Exact contract/PO reference (SKY-1819 is helpful).
- Formal PO line (if you have a PO number for the renewal).
- Primary procurement contact (we'll send the DocuSign and the Marketplace quote to them).
- Desired go-live date (you mentioned 2027-09-15).

Operationally we'll need to:
1) Map your current committed price to an Azure SKU (our Marketplace team will publish a private offer or map the existing SKU depending on your enterprise agreement).
2) Create the Marketplace listing/offer and assign entitlements to your tenant.
3) Issue a sample Marketplace invoice and a mutual action plan for the cutover.

I attached a copy of the current contract reference you mentioned for our team to confirm (attachment: contract_SKY-1819.pdf).

I'll bring Jordan (channel lead) and Grace (finance/program ops) into the thread to scope the enablement checklist and timelines. Expect a short internal outreach in the next couple hours to loop Microsoft.

Best,
Ava Chen
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com
Cell: +1-415-555-3287
Redwood Inference

Subject: Fwd: SKY-1819 — Marketplace conversion request (SkyBridge)

Team,

Forwarding SkyBridge's renewal request. They are a public-sector customer and must route renewals through Azure Marketplace starting this fiscal year. Key asks from procurement: preserve committed discount and provide an example invoice/AP file to satisfy their PO process.

What we need from Microsoft:
- Confirmation of whether this will be a private offer or standard plan.
- Marketplace SKU and publisher IDs so we can provision entitlements.
- Expected lead time for the offer to be visible to SkyBridge's tenant (they are targeting 2027-09-15).
- Any co-sell credits/eligibility impact for revenue recognition.

Grace/Vivek — can you confirm internal ops: are there any blocking accounting items for a marketplace renewal on a public-sector contract? Also include a sample invoice from Marketplace if available.

Deal: DBL-2041 / SKY-1819

Thanks,
Jordan Blake
Channel Partnerships | Redwood Inference
jordan.blake@redwood.com

From: Azure Marketplace Partner Team <mp_team@microsoft.com>

Cc: Azure Co-sell Desk <cosell@microsoft.com>, Timothy Hale <tim.hale@skybridge.gov>
Date: 2027-08-12T08:30:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Fwd: SKY-1819 — Marketplace conversion request (SkyBridge)

Thanks for reaching out and for the clear timeline. We can support a private offer that preserves committed pricing. High level next steps:

1) Publisher onboarding confirmation (we show Redwood's publisher id: RWD-778).
2) Create private offer referencing the existing SKU and provide a DocuSign quote linked to SkyBridge's tenant.
3) Once signed, Microsoft will provision the entitlement and SkyBridge will see the subscription in their tenant within 48–72 hours.

What we need from SkyBridge/Redwood:
- Copy of the purchase order and exact PO number.
- Confirmation of the billing entity (Microsoft invoice to SkyBridge or reseller invoicing).
- Any special PO fields required for AP.

Attached is an onboarding checklist and a sample invoice template. I'll also open a co-sell case to confirm any incentive eligibility.

Azure Marketplace Partner Team
mp_team@microsoft.com

Attached: MAP_SkyBridge_Aug2027.docx (mutual action plan / contact matrix). I will also add an example invoice once Grace confirms details.

If that timeline works, I can set a 20-minute kickoff call tomorrow afternoon to walk procurement and your AP team through the DocuSign and the sample invoice.

Best,
Ava Chen

title: Probe-weighted attribution request — July 29 partial degradation
preview:
From: Remy Ortega <remy@lentishealth.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Aug 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Impact on July 29 — end-to-end errors and SLA credit request

Hi Ben,

We saw a significant increase in 5xx errors from our production services between 2027-07-29T22:10Z and 2027-07-29T23:05Z (approximately 55 minutes). Our user-facing error rate spiked from ~0.2% to 6.8% and page load latencies doubled.

From our tracing, the failure appears to manifest as backend timeouts where Redwood endpoints returned 502/504 to our edge. We believe this is an availability event attributable to service path degradation and would like to request SLA credit for the impacted window.

I attached our frontend-errors.log and a short trace sample from our edge. Can you confirm Redwood’s view of the event and how you plan to attribute third-party CDN behavior vs Redwood’s serving layer?

We'd like this resolved quickly as this affected a large cohort of paying customers.

Thanks,
Remy Ortega
Head of SRE, Lentis Health
remy@lentishealth.io

Attachment: frontend-errors.log (excerpt)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Remy Ortega <remy@lentishealth.io>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Aug 2027 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Impact on July 29 — end-to-end errors and SLA credit request

Hi Remy — thanks for the quick heads-up and the logs. I’m sorry you saw that disruption. I’ve looped in Marcus from SRE and Marissa from CS so we can: 1) gather our probe/timeline evidence, 2) confirm attribution rules per your contract, and 3) get you a proposed credit calculation.

Immediate next steps:
- Marcus: can you pull the probe/timeline and a short correlation across our ingress/egress hops for 22:00–23:10Z?
- Marissa: I’ll draft a customer note and hold the contract reference on the apportionment method.

Remy — once we have the raw timelines we’ll propose a credit amount and explain the weighting. If you have additional traces (edge -> origin full spans) please attach them.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0198

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Remy Ortega <remy@lentishealth.io>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 9 Aug 2027 13:28:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Impact on July 29 — probe timeline and preliminary attribution

Hi all,

I pulled the internal probe timeline and correlated it with our egress telemetry. Summary of findings:
- Redwood service ingress (cpu/load, model hosts) shows nominal behavior during the window (no host OOMs, request queue depth stable).
- At ~2027-07-29T22:12Z we observe increased TCP retransmits and packet loss on the egress path toward the configured CDN edge (AS path: redwood -> upstream-transit -> acme-cdn). The loss pattern aligns with a sustained 40–60% packet retransmission rate for ~35 minutes, then tapering.
- Our application-layer probes saw elevated error rates starting at 22:10Z and peaking around 22:25Z, which aligns with the egress packet-loss spike.

Given our SLA apportionment policy (section 4.2), we apply a probe-weighted attribution model: if the upstream (Redwood-managed) path shows no internal service failure but the transit/3rd-party hop shows loss, we apportion partial responsibility. Based on probe density and error impact we have a preliminary apportionment of 40% Redwood / 60% third-party (ACME CDN).

I attached: redwood_probe_timeline.csv and a brief pcap-stub.txt with the observed retransmit summaries.

We understand that end-to-end user impact can feel like a full service outage; still, contract language requires us to disambiguate third-party segment responsibility. If Lentis has additional evidence showing internal host-level failures in our logs that we missed, we’ll re-evaluate.

—Marcus
Marcus Lin
Principal SRE, Redwood Inference
marcus_lin@redwood.com

Attachments: redwood_probe_timeline.csv, pcap-stub.txt

From: Remy Ortega <remy@lentishealth.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Aug 2027 08:16:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Impact on July 29 — probe timeline and preliminary attribution

Thanks Marcus — we appreciate the data. Two points of push-back from Lentis:

1) Our end-to-end traces show a near-100% correlation between our upstream requests to Redwood and user-facing failures during that minute range. For us the distinction between \"transit\" and \"service\" is academic if user requests are failing.
2) The SLA we rely on states availability measured end-to-end; paying customers will expect full remediation if the chain fails.

We can be pragmatic, but we’d like Redwood to consider a higher apportionment (at least 75%) to reflect end-user impact. If Redwood insists on 40% we will escalate to procurement/legal — we were counting on this credit for Q3 budgeting.

Can you re-run attribution with the traces I sent attached (I included more detailed edge spans this morning)?

—Remy


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Remy Ortega <remy@lentishealth.io>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 11 Aug 2027 15:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Impact on July 29 — probe-weighted attribution proposal

Remy — thank you for the clear feedback and for sharing the additional spans. I want to get this to a resolution without a formal escalation. Proposal below:

Summary of proposed resolution:
- Attribution methodology: weighted by probe correlation and user-impact window (we combine our probe timeline with Lentis end-to-end spans and weight by request volume during the highest-impact 5-minute rolling window).
- Proposed credit: 60% of the affected window credited to Lentis (compromise between our preliminary 40% and your 75%). This maps to a dollar credit of $4,500 against July invoice (calculation attached).
- Joint follow-up: a shared postmortem and a remediation plan with Redwood asking ACME CDN for their incident report and whether they will accept responsibility for the transit loss.

Attachments: credit_calc_proposal.xlsx (calc + methodology); I also linked SUP-2148 for tracking.

If that sounds acceptable we can route the credit immediately and schedule a 30-minute call Friday to review the joint postmortem. If not, please let me know your counter and we’ll engage Procurement.

Regards,
Ben

title: AtlasPay / Redwood: App-Bridge implementation & SDK onboarding
preview:
From: Devon Cole <devon.cole@atlaspay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 08 Aug 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: AtlasPay -> Redwood SDK onboarding request

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the product walkthrough last week. We're excited to move forward with Redwood for inference serving. High level: AtlasPay wants to replace our in-house text-generation endpoints with Redwood's hosted API and use the Redwood SDK for low-latency scoring in our payments UI.

Immediate asks:
- A kickoff and a proposed implementation checklist (SDK install, env setup, sample code).
- Guidance for endpoint migration: how to cut over traffic without dropping transactions.
- Retry/timeout patterns for our 99th‑pct latency spikes and recommended default backoff.
- Rate limit handling — we have bursty spikes around payroll windows (peak ~1200 req/s) and need a strategy (burst tokens, local queuing, fallback model).

Attached: current-traffic-metrics.csv (sanitized). Target go-live: early Sept (approx 3–4 week runway).

Questions: do we need a Dedicated pool or can we start on Hosted with pinning/burst configuration? Also, any required IP allowlists or auth steps we should prepare?

Thanks,
Devon
Devon Cole
Staff Engineering Manager, AtlasPay
devon.cole@atlaspay.com
+1 415-555-0183

--- attachment: current-traffic-metrics.csv (text/csv) ---

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Devon Cole <devon.cole@atlaspay.com>
Date: Mon, 09 Aug 2027 08:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AtlasPay -> Redwood SDK onboarding request

Hi Devon —

Great to hear. We can definitely support this timeline. Quick reply with a high-level plan and a couple of options so you can pick what matches your compliance/latency needs. I'll loop in our implementation lead for the technical checklist.

High-level recommendation (starter):
1) Start on Hosted API + SDK (fast start). If you need guaranteed throughput/SLA immediately, reserve a small Dedicated pool for peak windows and use smart routing to burst into Hosted if underutilized.
2) Pre-flight: provide example payloads, avg/peak token sizes, and allowlist IPs (we'll share Redwood IP ranges).
3) SDK setup: dev -> staging -> production with pinned model versions and a canary traffic split.
4) Endpoint migration: shadow traffic for 48–72h, run validation prompts, then incremental cutover (10% -> 25% -> 50% -> 100%).
5) Retries/timeouts: client-side 3 retries with exponential backoff (initial 100ms, 2x), and per-request deadline = client SLO - 100ms. Use idempotency keys where possible.
6) Rate limiting: token bucket on client + 429-aware retry with jitter. We'll provide local queuing guidance.

Proposed kickoff windows: Wed Aug 11 (09:00–11:00 PT) or Thu Aug 12 (13:00–15:00 PT). Which works for your team?

Looping in Monica Patel (implementation) and Kimberly Park (integrations) to own the technical checklist and SDK samples.

Regards,
Ben
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com

--- attachment: ben_onboarding_checklist.pdf (application/pdf) ---

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Cc: Devon Cole <devon.cole@atlaspay.com>
Date: Mon, 09 Aug 2027 08:17:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: AtlasPay -> Redwood SDK onboarding request

Monica / Kim —

Please take lead on the AtlasPay integration. Key items for the first pass:
- SDK quickstart: node + go samples, how to wire auth and env vars.
- Endpoint migration plan: shadowing, validation checks, rollback criteria.
- Retries/timeouts: recommended defaults and an example middleware for the SDK.
- Rate limit handling: guidance for client token-bucket and server-side quota coordination.
- Testing/observability: smoke tests, latency tracking, per-route dashboards.

Devon is targetting early Sept go-live and will provide payloads & traffic CSV. Can we have a runnable sample repo + checklist and a 60‑minute kickoff agenda by Tue EOD?

Thanks — Ben

On Mon, Aug 9, 2027 at 8:03 AM Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> Great to hear. We can definitely support this timeline....


From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
To: Devon Cole <devon.cole@atlaspay.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Aug 2027 10:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AtlasPay -> Redwood SDK onboarding request — Implementation checklist & samples

Hi Devon —

Thanks for the traffic file and the clear constraints. Below is our focused implementation cookbook and pragmatic defaults you can use to test immediately. I'll attach a small zip with runnable samples and a Postman collection.

1) SDK setup (faststart)
- Node (npm): npm install @redwood/inference
- Env: REDWOOD_API_KEY (short-lived for staging), REDWOOD_BASE_URL (use staging.redwood.example.com)
- Example (Node):
  const Redwood = require('@redwood/inference');
  const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY, baseUrl: process.env.REDWOOD_BASE_URL });
  
  async function score(text) {
    const res = await client.generate({ model: 'redwood-embed-1', prompt: text, maxTokens: 64, timeoutMs: 2200 });
    return res;
  }

- Example (Go):
  client := redwood.NewClient(os.Getenv("REDWOOD_API_KEY"))
  resp, err := client.Generate(context.Background(), &redwood.GenerateOpts{Model: "redwood-embed-1", Prompt: text, MaxTokens: 64})

2) Retries & timeouts (defaults)
- Client-side: 3 retries (on network error, 5xx, 429) with exponential backoff and full jitter.
- Backoff schedule example: 100ms, 250ms, 600ms (use randomized jitter ±25%).
- Per-request deadline: set timeoutMs = min(userSLO - 100ms, 3000ms) for UI scoring calls.
- Idempotency: attach request-id for non-idempotent multi-step ops.

3) Rate limit handling
- AtlasPay pattern: bursty payroll spikes. Server quotas will be per-API-key. Recommend:
  a) client token-bucket (burst 2x sustained rate with refill at sustained rate),
  b) on 429: immediate small backoff (randomized 100–500ms) then retry according to backoff schedule,
  c) queue depth cap: 250 requests per instance – if exceeded, fall back to a degraded local model or cached result.

4) Endpoint migration (safe cutover)
- Stage 0: Shadow (100% traffic to Redwood but responses ignored) for 48 hours. Compare latency and outputs; run acceptance prompts.
- Stage 1: Canary 10% (1–2h), monitor errors and latency.
- Stage 2: Incremental ramp (25% -> 50% -> 100%) with 1–4h observation windows.
- Rollback criteria: error rate > 1% over 15m OR tail latency >2x baseline.

5) Observability / validation
- Smoke tests: deterministic prompt set (10 prompts), compare golden responses; track similarity score and token consumption.
- Dashboards: per-route p50/p95/p99 latency, 429/5xx rates, cost per 1k tokens.
- Tracing: ensure request-id is propagated and visible in Redwood Console traces.

6) Local retries middleware (Node) — skeleton:
  async function withRetries(fn, opts) {
    let attempt = 0;
    const max = 3;
    while (attempt < max) {
      try {
        return await fn();
      } catch (err) {
        if (!shouldRetry(err)) throw err;
        const backoff = Math.floor(100 * Math.pow(2, attempt)) + Math.random() * 100;
        await sleep(backoff);
        attempt++;
      }
    }
    throw new Error('max retries exceeded');
  }

7) Testing checklist (for cutover)
- Run 48h shadow and automated similarity checks.
- Validate auth & allowlist.
- Run load test at 1.5x expected peak.
- Confirm canary rollback works end-to-end.

Attachments included: sdk-samples.zip (node + go samples), small Postman collection. Also linking a HubSpot record and DocuSign for contract signature (see links).

I'll be available on the kickoff. Proposed agenda (60m): intros (5m), infra & auth (10m), SDK demo (15m), migration plan & rollback (15m), Q&A & next actions (15m).

Cheers,
Monica Patel
Implementation Lead, Redwood Inference
monica@redwood.com

--- attachment: sdk-samples.zip (application/zip) ---

From: Devon Cole <devon.cole@atlaspay.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 12 Aug 2027 15:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AtlasPay -> Redwood SDK onboarding request — Implementation checklist & samples

Thanks Monica — this is perfect and actionable. Thu Aug 12 14:00 PT works for our team for the kickoff.

Before the call I've attached our allowlist and a small set of JWT SSO metadata you may need. We'll also provide a short set of production-like prompts for the shadow run.

Next immediate items we will provide:
- IP allowlist (attached).
- Short payload samples and avg token counts.
- Confirmation on whether we want initial Dedicated reservation (we'll confirm on the call).

One quick question on retries: should we bias towards more aggressive retries for idempotent scoring requests? Our UI applies scoring during checkout and misfires matter.

Look forward to the kickoff.

Best,
Devon
Devon Cole
Staff Engineering Manager, AtlasPay
devon.cole@atlaspay.com

6) Rate limiting: token bucket on client + 429-aware retry with jitter. We'll provide local queuing guidance.

Proposed kickoff windows: Wed Aug 11 (09:00–11:00 PT) or Thu Aug 12 (13:00–15:00 PT). Which works for your team?

Looping in Monica Patel (implementation) and Kimberly Park (integrations) to own the technical checklist and SDK samples.

Monica / Kim —

Please take lead on the AtlasPay integration. Key items for the first pass:

On Mon, Aug 9, 2027 at 8:03 AM Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com> wrote:
> Great to hear. We can definitely support this timeline....


From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
To: Devon Cole <devon.cole@atlaspay.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 10 Aug 2027 10:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: AtlasPay -> Redwood SDK onboarding request — Implementation checklist & samples

Hi Devon —

Thanks for the traffic file and the clear constraints. Below is our focused implementation cookbook and pragmatic defaults you can use to test immediately. I'll attach a small zip with runnable samples and a Postman collection.

1) SDK setup (faststart)
- Node (npm): npm install @redwood/inference
- Env: REDWOOD_API_KEY (short-lived for staging), REDWOOD_BASE_URL (use staging.redwood.example.com)
- Example (Node):
  const Redwood = require('@redwood/inference');
  const client = new Redwood({ apiKey: process.env.REDWOOD_API_KEY, baseUrl: process.env.REDWOOD_BASE_URL });
  
  async function score(text) {
    const res = await client.generate({ model: 'redwood-embed-1', prompt: text, maxTokens: 64, timeoutMs: 2200 });
    return res;
  }

- Example (Go):
  client := redwood.NewClient(os.Getenv("REDWOOD_API_KEY"))
  resp, err := client.Generate(context.Background(), &redwood.GenerateOpts{Model: "redwood-embed-1", Prompt: text, MaxTokens: 64})

2) Retries & timeouts (defaults)
- Client-side: 3 retries (on network error, 5xx, 429) with exponential backoff and full jitter.
- Backoff schedule example: 100ms, 250ms, 600ms (use randomized jitter ±25%).
- Per-request deadline: set timeoutMs = min(userSLO - 100ms, 3000ms) for UI scoring calls.
- Idempotency: attach request-id for non-idempotent multi-step ops.

3) Rate limit handling
- AtlasPay pattern: bursty payroll spikes. Server quotas will be per-API-key. Recommend:
  a) client token-bucket (burst 2x sustained rate with refill at sustained rate),
  b) on 429: immediate small backoff (randomized 100–500ms) then retry according to backoff schedule,
  c) queue depth cap: 250 requests per instance – if exceeded, fall back to a degraded local model or cached result.

4) Endpoint migration (safe cutover)
- Stage 0: Shadow (100% traffic to Redwood but responses ignored) for 48 hours. Compare latency and outputs; run acceptance prompts.
- Stage 1: Canary 10% (1–2h), monitor errors and latency.
- Stage 2: Incremental ramp (25% -> 50% -> 100%) with 1–4h observation windows.
- Rollback criteria: error rate > 1% over 15m OR tail latency >2x baseline.

5) Observability / validation
- Smoke tests: deterministic prompt set (10 prompts), compare golden responses; track similarity score and token consumption.
- Dashboards: per-route p50/p95/p99 latency, 429/5xx rates, cost per 1k tokens.
- Tracing: ensure request-id is propagated and visible in Redwood Console traces.

6) Local retries middleware (Node) — skeleton:
  async function withRetries(fn, opts) {
    let attempt = 0;
    const max = 3;
    while (attempt < max) {
      try {
        return await fn();
      } catch (err) {
        if (!shouldRetry(err)) throw err;
        const backoff = Math.floor(100 * Math.pow(2, attempt)) + Math.random() * 100;
        await sleep(backoff);
        attempt++;
      }
    }
    throw new Error('max retries exceeded');
  }

7) Testing checklist (for cutover)
- Run 48h shadow and automated similarity checks.
- Validate auth & allowlist.
- Run load test at 1.5x expected peak.
- Confirm canary rollback works end-to-end.

Attachments included: sdk-samples.zip (node + go samples), small Postman collection. Also linking a HubSpot record and DocuSign for contract signature (see links).

I'll be available on the kickoff. Proposed agenda (60m): intros (5m), infra & auth (10m), SDK demo (15m), migration plan & rollback (15m), Q&A & next actions (15m).

Cheers,
Monica Patel
Implementation Lead, Redwood Inference
monica@redwood.com

[Attachment: scrubbed-audit-snippet.txt (txt)]

Infra note (copyable):
\"Redwood Dedicated: by default, snapshot and backup assets are created in the same physical region as the host cluster and are not replicated to other regions unless cross-region replication is explicitly configured by the customer. The Dedicated control plane supports optional export (push) to secondary regions or third-party archival; these flows require explicit enrollment and are not part of the default provisioning. For purges, both primary region artifacts and any configured exports must be addressed — the runbook enumerates discovery steps to find such exports.\"

From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Alex Wang <alex.wang@skylinehealth.ai>
Date: Fri, 30 Jul 2027 10:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Renewal questions — premium SLA and committed throughput

Ben / Alex — technical addendum from infra: 

- 99th pct targets are conditional on model configuration (sequence length distribution) and KV cache hit rate. For Skyline’s typical prompts (~120 tokens median), targeted 99th pct of 200ms is realistic on dedicated RTX-class pools with continuous batching and prefix caching. If your long prompts (>512 tokens) are >10% of traffic, expect tail to widen; advise token cap guidance or prompt truncation for canary.
- Autoscaling & burst: We recommend 1.5x committed burst via short-lived transient workers; this requires a 48-hour advance notice window for large planned events >3x normal traffic.
- Oncall: We’ll allocate two dedicated SRE rotations during the 2-week ramp window to reduce MTTD and coordinate with Skyline’s oncall.

I’ll attach the earlier internal runbook and then we can convert relevant sections into the SLA addendum.

Evelyn Hart
Director, Infrastructure Reliability | Redwood Inference

Dedicated capacity request — geo-hybrid pool sizing for Helios AI
preview:
From: Elena Torres <elena@helios.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@helios.ai>
Date: 2027-08-01T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Initial capacity ask — global footprint

Hi Alex,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Helios is moving to an enterprise contract and we need Redwood to support a geo-hybrid dedicated footprint (US + EU + APAC) to meet latency and data locality requirements. High level asks: 1) recommended reserved pool sizing per region for our expected traffic profile, 2) burst/backstop strategy for unpredictable model spikes, 3) onboarding timeline for APAC (we're targeting a Singapore pilot).

I've attached a spreadsheet with estimated QPS/peak hours per model and the mix of models (2x large-chat, 1x embedding-heavy, 1x reranker). We can be flexible with quantization if it helps reduce capacity needs — want your recommendation. Procurement looped in on timeline and PO cadence.

Can we get a preliminary sizing and a short proposal this week? We're aiming to sign by 08/20 if numbers align.

Thanks,
Elena
VP Product, Helios AI
--Attachment: Helios_requirements.xlsx (spreadsheet)--

From: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Torres <elena@helios.ai>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-08-01T12:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Initial capacity ask — global footprint

Hi Elena — thanks for the detail and the spreadsheet. I've looped in Lauren (sales lead), Marcus (capacity planning) and Hannah (APAC ops) so we can produce a working sizing and timeline. Quick follow-ups so our calc matches your goals:

- Can you confirm 99th percentile latency SLOs for chat vs embeddings (target ms)?
- Do you expect traffic to be stable cross-region (e.g., 40/35/25 split) or concentrated in certain markets during business hours?
- Any regulatory data residency constraints per model (EU-only, APAC-only)?
- Are you open to a staged rollout (reserve smaller initial pool + burst lanes) or do you need full reserved capacity at sign?

I propose Marcus and I turn around a preliminary region-by-region sizing today with three scenarios (conservative, baseline, lean) and a suggested sprint plan for APAC onboarding. Can we carve 30 minutes tomorrow to walk through?

Best,
Alex Martinez
Senior Sales Engineer, Redwood Inference
--Attachment: (none)--

From: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-08-02T08:22:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Re: Initial capacity ask — global footprint

Team — forwarding Helios thread. I drafted a quick calc (attached) for our baseline scenario based on Elena's sheet. Key assumptions are in-line: 60% peak concurrency on chat models, 25% embeddings load, 15% reranker/batch jobs. I'm flagging two decision points for the customer: allow 4-bit/8-bit quantization for the chat family, and confirm APAC pilot node class (A100 vs L40s). If customer rejects quantization we need ~30% more reserved GPUs.

Please review Marcus — especially the APAC lead times (Hannah) and put together a recommended pool split (US/EU/APAC) and burst lane plan.

--Attachment: prelim_pool_calc.csv (CSV) --

From: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.ai>
To: Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai>, Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah_schmitt@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-08-02T11:40:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Re: Initial capacity ask — global footprint

On Tue, Aug 2, 2027 at 08:22 AM Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Team — forwarding Helios thread. I drafted a quick calc (attached) for our baseline scenario based on Elena's sheet.

Thanks — ran three scenarios and here's our recommendation (numbers are reserved GPU-equivalents using our L40s profile):

- Baseline (preferred for signing): US 36, EU 24, APAC 18 reserved; burst lanes: 25% on top (auto-burst to shared pool + queued fallback).
- Conservative: US 50, EU 34, APAC 26 (for hard-SLA workloads; adds ~28% monthly cost).
- Lean (pilot-first): US 22, EU 14, APAC 8 (start small, 12-week ramp triggered by traffic thresholds).

Tradeoffs:
- Quantization to 8-bit reduces reserved GPUs by ~18%; 4-bit is aggressive but viable for some chat flows.
- APAC lead time for dedicated nodes in Singapore with our partner is ~6–8 weeks to provision + validation; can be shortened to 3–4 weeks with accelerated on-prem staging and prioritization (one-time onboarding surcharge).
- Recommend an initial contractual commitment for baseline with a 12-week canary in APAC to validate live traffic. After that, auto-scale policies can handle burst.

Links: sizing workbook + cost lines -> https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1aB2cHeliosSizing
DocuSign template for enterprise terms: https://app.docusign.com/templates/Helios-Enterprise-Template

I'll attach the breakdown CSV to share with Elena.

Marcus
Capacity Planner, Redwood Inference
--Attachment: pool_split_breakdown.xlsx (spreadsheet)--

From: Elena Torres <elena@helios.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex_martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@helios.ai>, Ops Contact <ops@codalabs.ai>
Date: 2027-08-04T14:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Initial capacity ask — global footprint

Alex / team — thanks for the quick turnaround and the clarity. A few notes from procurement and ops: 

- Procurement prefers the Baseline plan for initial commit (US 36 / EU 24 / APAC 18) but asks if we can shift 6 APAC GPUs into an accelerated pilot window. In short: we need Singapore pilot capacity within 4 weeks for an EMEA+APAC launch readiness demo.
- Ops (Codalabs) requests that the APAC nodes be deployed in a private VPC with customer-managed KMS for the pilot.
- We are okay with 8-bit quantization on chat if Redwood certifies no material quality regression on our evaluation prompts.

Procurement timeline: internal PO approval expected by 08/12; final signature by 08/20. Can Redwood provide a firm APAC onboarding ETA and any one-time onboarding fees so we can include in the PO?

Best,
Elena
VP Product, Helios AI
--Attachment: apac_pilot_scope.docx (doc)--

Can you provide a 1–2 page letter or a short PDF we can include in the package? If possible we'd like it by EOD Friday, 8/6. If you need to point us to an existing attestation or include additional evidence (SOC2 PDF / controls change log), please attach.

procurement@borealis.ai

title: Post-call: Eastbridge TCO & pilot levers (batching / KV cache)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anika Sharma <anika.sharma@eastbridgecap.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 09 Aug 2027 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call: Eastbridge TCO & pilot levers (batching / KV cache)

Thanks again for your time today, Anika — appreciated the transparency on budget and the latency targets. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we’re aligned.

Key pain & goals (as we heard):
- 1) High volume authorization checks (~600 qps peak) causing direct cost pressure and occasional latency spikes during spikes of traffic.
- 2) Regulatory data retention requirement—need clear isolation and auditability for model inputs.
- 3) Target: pilot to prove 40–60% reduced per-transaction token spend while keeping 95th percentile latency <= 280ms.

Cost levers we discussed:
- Batching windows: tradeoff between micro-batching at 50ms vs larger 200ms windows (we estimate ~18–28% token cost reduction for typical traffic shape).
- KV/prefix caching: for repeated authorization tokens, can cut tokenized prompt length by up to ~40% for high locality routes.
- Quantized model variant for inference in Dedicated mode: ~15–20% unit-cost saving with minimal quality loss in our bench.

Attached: EB_TCO_Snapshot_v1.xlsx — quick TCO sketch using your auth qps and 1.2 tokens/txn median; assumptions tab included.

Proposed pilot scope & milestones:
1) Data & compliance check (this week) — procurement & security exchange SOC2 report + retention plan.
2) 2-week integration + warm-up on Redwood Dedicated (small pool) to validate latency profile.
3) 3-week pilot ramp to 10% traffic with batching + prefix cache enabled; measure token/unit costs and latency SLOs.

Asks from you by EOD Wed:
- Confirm procurement contact for a quick security packet exchange (we’ll need the SOC2 and any custom DPA items).
- Approve motion to run the initial pilot on a reserved small pool (we’ve priced the pilot assumptions in the attached spreadsheet).

Next internal steps (cc’ing Marissa & Hannah): please see pilot SOW draft attached (redwood-pilot-sow-draft.pdf). I’ll stand up a short decision checklist after Anika confirms procurement.

Thanks —
Ben
Ben Carter | Solutions Lead, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0173

Attachment: EB_TCO_Snapshot_v1.xlsx (Workbook, 92 KB)
Attachment: redwood-pilot-sow-draft.pdf (PDF, 48 KB)

From: Anika Sharma <anika.sharma@eastbridgecap.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwoodinference.com>, Eastbridge Capital Procurement <procurement@eastbridgecap.com>
Date: Mon, 09 Aug 2027 11:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: Eastbridge TCO & pilot levers (batching / KV cache)

Ben — thanks for the clear recap. Confirming a few points from our side:
- Budget: we have an initial pilot allocation of $45k to validate economics; any pilot pricing above that will need exec approval.
- Latency: our internal SLA target for auth flow is 300ms P95 end-to-end; anything above that will need a fallback plan to previous infra.
- Data: procurement will want see SOC2 & explanation on KV cache retention windows (we cannot persist raw PII beyond 7 days).

Procurement is on this thread — procurement@eastbridgecap.com. I’ll get them the attached usage estimates and ask them to confirm whether $45k covers the pilot.

Quick clarification: when you say \"40–60% reduced per-transaction token spend,\" is that net of batching + caching + quantized model? Would appreciate a single-line summary of the assumptions you used.

Appreciate the SOW draft — we’ll review and get back with redlines.

—Anika
Anika Sharma | Head of Product, Eastbridge Capital
anika.sharma@eastbridgecap.com | +1 (646) 555-0139

Attachment: EB_auth_qps_export.csv (CSV, 22 KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Anika Sharma <anika.sharma@eastbridgecap.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 09 Aug 2027 11:25:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: Eastbridge TCO & pilot levers (batching / KV cache)

Great questions, Anika — short answers below and I’ll add the single-line assumption into the spreadsheet summary tab.

One-line net assumption: combined effect of conservative batching (50–100ms window), prefix/KV caching for high-locality tokens, and using a 4-bit quantized model variant yields a modeled 42% reduction in median token usage per auth flow and ~36% reduction in expected billed tokens after accounting for batching inefficiencies and fallbacks. (See assumptions tab, rows 12–18.)

Important caveats:
- That \"net\" number is based on your median 1.2 tokens/txn figure; if real median >>2.0 tokens/txn or locality is low, savings compress toward ~18–22%.
- Latency risk: micro-batching at 50ms is low-impact; 200ms windows will change P95 materially. We will measure P95 on the Dedicated pool and can auto-fallback to single-request mode when SLOs are breached.

Actionables I’ll take now:
- Update the TCO spreadsheet with the single-line summary and three scenario tabs (base / optimistic / conservative).
- Prepare a short \"pilot guardrails\" doc that defines caching retention, anonymization, and fallback triggers for the first rollout.

Can we schedule a 30-min procurement/security sync Thu 10–11am PT? If that works I’ll get an invite to procurement@eastbridgecap.com and Anika.

Thanks —
Ben

Attachment: EB_TCO_Snapshot_v1.xlsx (Workbook, updated 115 KB)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Hannah Schmitt <hannah.schmitt@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 09 Aug 2027 11:35:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Post-call: Eastbridge TCO & pilot levers (batching / KV cache)

Ben / Hannah — quick internal note:
- I reviewed the spreadsheet; with their $45k pilot cap, we should scope to a 3-week ramp at ~10% traffic and instrument early quality checks (synthetic + live sample).
- Pricing ask: propose we offer a capped pilot fee of $38k (covers Dedicated pool + engineering runbook + up to 3 adjustments) and keep $7k as optional sizing buffer if they push to 20% traffic in pilot.
- Need Hannah to OK the procurement sync agenda (SOC2, retained data policy, DPA redline path).

I’ll prepare the one-pager on acceptance criteria so procurement has something concise.

—Marissa

Great questions, Anika — short answers below and I’ll add the single-line assumption into the spreadsheet summary tab.

One-line net assumption: combined effect of conservative batching (50–100ms window), prefix/KV caching for high-locality tokens, and using a 4-bit quantized model variant yields a modeled 42% reduction in median token usage per auth flow and ~36% reduction in expected billed tokens after accounting for batching inefficiencies and fallbacks. (See assumptions tab, rows 12–18.)

Important caveats:
- That \"net\" number is based on your median 1.2 tokens/txn figure; if real median >>2.0 tokens/txn or locality is low, savings compress toward ~18–22%.
- Latency risk: micro-batching at 50ms is low-impact; 200ms windows will change P95 materially. We will measure P95 on the Dedicated pool and can auto-fallback to single-request mode when SLOs are breached.

Actionables I’ll take now:
- Update the TCO spreadsheet with the single-line summary and three scenario tabs (base / optimistic / conservative).
- Prepare a short \"pilot guardrails\" doc that defines caching retention, anonymization, and fallback triggers for the first rollout.

Can we schedule a 30-min procurement/security sync Thu 10–11am PT? If that works I’ll get an invite to procurement@eastbridgecap.com and Anika.

Thanks —
Ben

Attachment: EB_TCO_Snapshot_v1.xlsx (Workbook, updated 115 KB)

title: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options
preview:
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 03 Aug 2027 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Hi Samir —

Thanks again for the time today. Quick write-up of what we covered and two recommended paths for credentials so your security and procurement teams can evaluate quickly.

Recap (short):
- Demo walk-through: Redwood hosted API + optional Dedicated pool for production throughput.
- Key integration points for Acme: SDK bootstrap in backend services, browser-safe flows for clinicians, embeddings for semantic search, and a re-ranker for prioritization.

Credentials options we recommend (tradeoffs):
1) Service-account JWT (recommended for backend services)
   - Description: Use JWT-signed service account credentials with short-lived bearer tokens issued by Redwood’s token service.
   - Pros: Strong auditability, supports automated rotation via KMS, easy to integrate with server-side SDKs, works well for embedding + reranker calls.
   - Cons: Requires a one-time provisioning step and KMS/HSM integration for private key storage if you require hardware-backed keys.

2) API keys + short-lived session tokens for browser use (where needed)
   - Description: Issued from a backend exchange endpoint. Do not embed static API keys in client apps.
   - Pros: Simpler initial dev experience, lower friction for early POCs.
   - Cons: Higher operational risk if used as long-lived keys; procurement will likely push for rotation and scoped keys.

Attachments: sample SDK bootstrapping file (redwood-sdk-sample-config.yaml) and an initial SOW draft attached for procurement review.

Next steps (proposed):
- You confirm which credential approach you prefer (JWT service account vs API-key-first) — target by EOD Thu 2027-08-05.
- If JWT: schedule a 30-min security call to cover KMS/HSM and audit log access (we can include our security engineer).
- If API keys: we will provide a short-lived token exchange example and extra guardrails for key rotation cadence.

Would love to loop procurement/contact person when you’re ready. I cc’d Marissa for pricing implications.

Thanks,
Alex Martinez
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
redwood.ai

Attachments: redwood-sdk-sample-config.yaml (text/yaml), acme-sow-draft.pdf (application/pdf)


From: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Nora Li <nora.li@procureops.com>
Date: Wed, 04 Aug 2027 09:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Alex —

Great summary, thanks. A couple quick clarifying Qs from our security/procurement side: 

- Rotation cadence: For the JWT approach, what is the recommended key rotation interval and do you support automated rotation via a cloud KMS (we use GCP KMS)?
- HSM requirement: Procurement indicated they prefer HSM-backed keys for production secrets. Can Redwood's token service accept public keys stored in an external KMS/HSM or do we need to host the signing service?
- Data access model: When we call embeddings and reranker, where does the model execution happen and what logs are retained? We need an audit trail for queries and a retention policy.

Also: we prefer to avoid embedding static API keys in any client. We will likely want the JWT approach for server-to-server flows and a browser short-lived-exchange for clinician UIs.

I can put Nora (procurement) on a thread for SOW and compliance docs. Please share any SOC2 / audit evidence links.

Thanks,
Samir Shah
Head of Engineering, Acme Health
samir@acmehealth.com

---
Acme is HIPAA-conscious; all PII/PHI must be handled per our security checklist.


From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 05 Aug 2027 10:28:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Samir —

Answers inline below. Short version: JWT service account + KMS-backed private keys is the recommended path for production; we support it and can map to your GCP KMS.

- Rotation cadence: Recommended 30–90 days for signing keys; we support automated rotation hooks and can integrate with GCP KMS for key lifecycle. We also support issuing short-lived tokens (15m to 1h) to callers so the signing key rotation is decoupled from token lifetime.
- HSM: Redwood supports external KMS/HSM integrations. For GCP KMS we can store the signing key material in KMS and configure Redwood to call KMS-based signing. If procurement requires a fully customer-managed signing service (air-gapped or on-prem), our Private deployment path supports that.
- Logs & retention: For hosted API, we retain audit logs (request ids, timestamps, model variant, token identity) for 90 days by default; we can extend retention for enterprise contracts. Logs do not contain raw PHI by default — customers can enable encrypted request/response logging if needed and agree on an encrypted storage lifecycle.

Attachments: redwood-rotation-playbook.pdf (explains rotation, token lifetimes, and example KMS config).

Implementation notes / SDK bits:
- Backend: use our server SDK (see sample config attached) to exchange a service-account JWT for a short-lived bearer token used for all Redwood API calls.
- Browser flows: implement a backend token-exchange endpoint that mints ephemeral scoped tokens for clinicians (no long-lived keys in client).
- Embeddings/rerank: call with the service token; we recommend scoping embedding model calls to a dedicated model alias and enabling prefix caching for performance.

If this matches your expected security posture, we can:
1) Prepare a revised SOW reflecting KMS/HSM and extended log retention (I attached the SOW draft).
2) Schedule a 30-min security deep-dive with our security engineer (propose Fri 2027-08-06 10:00 PDT).

Next step for you: confirm preference for JWT-based service accounts and whether to include GCP KMS integration in the SOW.

—Alex


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 06 Aug 2027 08:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Samir —

Looping in Ben from Solutions/Infra to flag cost and dedicated capacity implications. Quick notes on commercial side: 
- If you choose JWT + KMS-backed keys and require 90-day log retention + dedicated GPU pool, we will include a Dedicated proposal in the SOW (monthly committed throughput).
- If you want hosted with standard retention, we can do a lower-touch plan and shorten commercial lead time.

Ben, can you share a ballpark for dedicated pool pricing and any lead times for a GCP-region deployment? Samir, happy to work with Nora on procurement and finalize the SOW.

Thanks,
Marissa Cole
Head of Sales | Redwood Inference



From: Samir Shah <samir@acmehealth.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>
Cc: Nora Li <nora.li@procureops.com>
Date: Fri, 06 Aug 2027 12:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo: Acme Health - credentials & integration decision options

Marissa / Alex —

Thanks all. We prefer to proceed with the JWT service-account approach integrated with our GCP KMS for production (HSM-backed keys). Please include:
- KMS integration and signing details in the SOW
- 90-day audit log retention and optional export to our SIEM
- Scoped tokens for browser token exchange pattern (no static client keys)

Procurement contact: Nora Li (nora.li@procureops.com). Please send the updated SOW and I will shepherd it through approval. We are ready to sign once the KMS and retention items are explicit.

DocuSign link for quick turnaround: https://docusign.example.com/ACM-1342-sow

Samir Shah

Hi Camila,

Thanks for the quick onboarding call this morning. Per our discussion, attaching the completed W-9 and the bank authorization form. We need Redwood to confirm the PO field Redwood will place on invoices and the exact remit string so our AR team can automate payment posting.

Attachments:
- sapphire_w9.pdf (W-9)
- sapphire_bank_authorization.pdf (signed ACH form)

A few quick questions from our side:
1) What PO format should we include on each invoice? (example: RWD-2027-####)

From: Camila Reyes <camila_reyes@redwood.com>
To: Ari Patel <ari.patel@sapphire-supply.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 3 Aug 2027 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Vendor banking setup and PO mapping request

Hi Ari — great to meet earlier. Thanks for sending the forms. I’ve looped in Ben from FinOps and Marissa from Vendor Ops to finalize the payment details. Next steps below:

1) Banking validation: Ben will initiate a micro-deposit verification (2 small deposits) to the account in the bank authorization form. Please confirm when you see them and reply with the exact amounts.

2) PO format: Our procurement system assigns PO numbers like RWD-2027-3381. Please include "PO: RWD-2027-####" on the invoice header and in the PDF metadata (if possible). If you can include a separate line in the invoice CSV export as "po_number" that’s ideal.

preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn@redwood.com>, Susan Park <susan@acme.com>

Date: Tue, 3 Aug 2027 09:15:00 -0700
Subject: Token saturation & load profile review — propose times

Hi team / Susan,

Thanks for the quick intro call yesterday. Per ACME's ask, they'd like a focused run-through of the token-saturation characteristics we've observed and agree on a load-test profile (targets: sustained 1k TPS with 512 token context, 25ms p50 generation). Goal is to align on:
- workload shape (batch sizes, concurrency)
- canary / staging expectations
- success criteria for the demo (latency SLOs + tail behavior)

Proposed slots (PT):
- Wed 8/4 10:00–11:30 (90m)
- Wed 8/4 15:30–17:00 (90m)
- Thu 8/5 08:00–09:30 (90m, APAC friendly)

Attach: load-profile.csv (small sample) and initial-run-notes.pdf.

If we need the runtime team in, Karthik, can you confirm whether 90 minutes is sufficient to run a short staged load and walk through kernel-level metrics? Laura / Marcus / Evelyn - any must-have slides or runbook sections?

Thanks,

Ben Carter
Sr. Sales Engineer | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0123
Redwood Inference

Attachments: load-profile.csv (CSV), initial-run-notes.pdf (PDF)

Agree 90 minutes is the right cadence. Suggest the session agenda:
1) 15m — telemetry walk (traces + device-level metrics)
2) 25m — baseline load test (small, replicate ACME-like payloads)
3) 25m — saturation test (ramping until KV pressure / token queueing shows)
4) 15m — mitigation paths (batching tweaks, quantization profile, fallback routing)

Two operational asks:
- Can ACME provide a representative prompt set + token distribution? (if not we can use the attached load-profile.csv sample)
- Which harness should we use for saturation runs? We can use our in-house runner (preferred) or JMeter if ACME needs to own the client-side.

Marcus / Laura — please bring SLA targets you want surfaced in the runbook.

— Karthik

Principal Infrastructure Engineer | Redwood Inference

title: GPU pool onboarding — regional prioritization & burst policy
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ken Lee <ken@alpineanalytics.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Sun, 1 Aug 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: GPU pool onboarding — regional prioritization & burst policy

Ken —

Thanks again for the call yesterday. Per our discussion I've started a concise onboarding plan so we can lock down the GPU pool sizing, autoscaling/burst behavior, and regional priorities before procurement. Quick ask: can you confirm the expected concurrent request profile and any data residency constraints? That will determine whether we need a dedicated EU failover pool or simply routing + logging separation.

High-level proposal (draft):
- Primary region: us-west (GCP us-west2) for prod traffic.
- Secondary (failover): eu-west (GCP europe-west1) for EU customers / residency.
- Pool spec (initial): 8x A100-80GB-equivalent GPUs (can mix A30/A100 depending on availability).
- Autoscaling: base capacity = 4 GPUs, burst capacity = up to 8 GPUs for spikes. Burst nodes spin up to 90s, pre-warm option available for steady ramp.
- Cost control: burst TTL 15 minutes, cool-down 10 minutes; prefix/KV cache retained in primary region, cross-region fallback uses host-model (smaller instance) for safety.

Attachments: capacity_requirements.xlsx (high-level QPS / concurrency table) — please fill the green column with realistic 95th and 99th percentile token-per-second numbers.

Next steps I propose:
1) You confirm regional residency and peak concurrency (reply inline).
2) Redwood will produce a provisioning checklist plus a proposed activation window.
3) Procurement loop (we'll cc your procurement contact) and a short SOW addendum for Dedicated capacity.

If this looks reasonable I'll loop in Monica to start the provisioning checklist and Marissa to schedule the activation slot.

Best,

Ben Carter
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
ben@redwoodinference.com | +1 (415) 555-0198

--- attachment stub: capacity_requirements.xlsx (spreadsheet) ---

From: Ken Lee <ken@alpineanalytics.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, procurement@alpineanalytics.com
Date: Mon, 2 Aug 2027 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: GPU pool onboarding — regional prioritization & burst policy

Ben —

Thanks for the clear breakdown. A few clarifications from our side:
- Concurrency: steady-state is ~50 concurrent LLM sessions across our main product (each session ~1–2 requests/sec, median 128 tokens). 95th percentile traffic windows push to ~180 concurrent sessions.
- Residency: EU customers' data must remain in EU for storage and logging; inference can be routed out of EU if no PII is present, but we prefer a regional failover. So secondary pool in eu-west is required.
- Timeline: procurement will need ~2 weeks after we validate sizing; implementation + activation targeting ~3 weeks thereafter (so ~5 weeks end-to-end).

Procurement (procurement@alpineanalytics.com) — I'll need a short SOW addendum and an estimated month-1 invoice line item for reserved baseline capacity.

Happy to sync if any of the numbers above affect the proposed pool spec.

—Ken
Ken Lee
VP Product — Alpine Analytics
ken@alpineanalytics.com | +1 (212) 555-0102

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 2 Aug 2027 13:18:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: GPU pool onboarding — regional prioritization & burst policy

Marissa / Monica — forwarding Ken's reply. TL;DR: steady 50 conc, 95th ~180; EU failover required for logs/storage; procurement ~2 weeks.

Monica — can you draft the provisioning checklist and provide an initial node spec that fits 50–200 concurrent sessions? Suggest we design for headroom: base 4 GPUs, burst to 8 GPUs, but show a cost variant for 6 base / 10 burst as an alternative. Include pre-warm (yes/no) cost delta.

Marissa — please start a proposed activation timeline and procurement checklist items (SOW addendum, invoice cadence). I'll own the customer comms.

Key questions for infra/ops:
- For cross-region failover, do we do a cold failover (spin up eu pool on demand) or keep a minimal hot pool (1–2 GPUs) to reduce warm-up latency?
- Is cross-region KV/cache replication required (we should avoid it given consistency issues) — prefer separate caches with fallback indicated in routing policy.

Attachments: provisioning-checklist.md, sla-exceptions-request.pdf (stubs).

Ben

--- attachment stub: provisioning-checklist.md ---
--- attachment stub: sla-exceptions-request.pdf ---

From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 3 Aug 2027 09:04:00 -07:00
Subject: FW: GPU pool onboarding — regional prioritization & burst policy

Rishi — forwarding Alpine's numbers and Ben's proposed model. Two small asks before we finalize the provisioning request to infra:
1) Autoscale thresholds — Ben suggested base 4 GPUs, burst to 8 GPUs, burst TTL 15m. Ops preference historically is to require a higher trigger (e.g., scale when queue > 60% for 2 consecutive minutes) to avoid noisy up/down cycles. Can you confirm an acceptable threshold and whether we should gate burst to only non-authenticated traffic (if that's even feasible)?
2) Pre-warm behavior — for predictable ramps (we expect a marketing-driven spike during product rollout) should we pre-warm 1 GPU 10 minutes before activation and then allow burst to fill? That reduces cold-start tail latency but increases cost.

Inline copy of Ken's note for context:
> \"steady-state is ~50 concurrent LLM sessions ... 95th percentile traffic windows push to ~180 concurrent sessions.\"

Thanks — we want to lock this today so procurement has the SOW line items.

Marissa
Sr. Customer Success Manager — Redwood Inference
marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi_malhotra@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 3 Aug 2027 15:41:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FW: GPU pool onboarding — regional prioritization & burst policy

All —

I reviewed the numbers and the proposed approach. Approve the baseline plan with two adjustments:
1) Lower burst trigger: scale up burst capacity when sustained queue > 45% for 90s (this gives us fewer oscillations but responds faster than 2 minutes).
2) Pre-warm policy: enable 1 pre-warm GPU at T-minus 10 minutes for scheduled activations only (we don't want continuous pre-warm on unscheduled spikes). Implement pre-warm billing as a flagged line in the SOW.

Operational notes for provisioning ticket:
- Node type: prefer a mix strategy (A30 for steady base nodes, A100-equivalent for burst nodes). List both flavors in the provisioning ticket so hardware can be assigned by region.
- KV/cache: do not replicate cross-region; use separate caches and routing failover to host-model (smaller, quantized) for EU if needed.

Marissa / Monica — with this I think you can finalize the checklist and SOW addendum. Ben — please confirm to Ken and cc procurement that this is the plan and the estimated go-live window (3 weeks from procurement sign-off).

Rishi
Director, Platform Operations — Redwood Inference
rishi_malhotra@redwoodinference.com

Subject: FW: GPU pool onboarding — regional prioritization & burst policy

Rishi — forwarding Alpine's numbers and Ben's proposed model. Two small asks before we finalize the provisioning request to infra:
1) Autoscale thresholds — Ben suggested base 4 GPUs, burst to 8 GPUs, burst TTL 15m. Ops preference historically is to require a higher trigger (e.g., scale when queue > 60% for 2 consecutive minutes) to avoid noisy up/down cycles. Can you confirm an acceptable threshold and whether we should gate burst to only non-authenticated traffic (if that's even feasible)?

Date: Tue, 3 Aug 2027 15:41:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: FW: GPU pool onboarding — regional prioritization & burst policy

All —

I reviewed the numbers and the proposed approach. Approve the baseline plan with two adjustments:
1) Lower burst trigger: scale up burst capacity when sustained queue > 45% for 90s (this gives us fewer oscillations but responds faster than 2 minutes).
2) Pre-warm policy: enable 1 pre-warm GPU at T-minus 10 minutes for scheduled activations only (we don't want continuous pre-warm on unscheduled spikes). Implement pre-warm billing as a flagged line in the SOW.

Operational notes for provisioning ticket:
- Node type: prefer a mix strategy (A30 for steady base nodes, A100-equivalent for burst nodes). List both flavors in the provisioning ticket so hardware can be assigned by region.
- KV/cache: do not replicate cross-region; use separate caches and routing failover to host-model (smaller, quantized) for EU if needed.

Marissa / Monica — with this I think you can finalize the checklist and SOW addendum. Ben — please confirm to Ken and cc procurement that this is the plan and the estimated go-live window (3 weeks from procurement sign-off).

Rishi

Elena — thanks for the clear asks. Looping Marissa (bench team) and Ben (sales ops) into the thread so we can lock the MAP milestones. Below is our proposed MAP summary (we'll put this into a formal MAP PDF and share to HubSpot):

Proposed MAP milestones (fast-track, 10 business days):

3) Day 3–5: Customer-run evaluation in Stella staging account using Redwood test harness (we provide container + config)

Acceptance metrics (initial proposal):
- TTFT (streaming enabled, first token delivered): p50 <= 70ms, p95 <= 210ms, p99 <= 420ms
- Streaming latency (inter-token for first 2 tokens perceived by client): p50 <= 35ms, p95 <= 150ms, p99 <= 300ms
- End-to-end completion latency for 180-token median: p50 <= 650ms, p95 <= 1100ms, p99 <= 1600ms
- Concurrency target: steady 12 concurrent sessions, 95% requests should meet p95 targets above

Test harness & config we will provide:
- Containerized harness (k6 + custom streaming client) configured to Stella token distribution
- Pre-built model variant (quantized where appropriate) and kernel-config for matching latency profile
- KV-cache prefixing to mirror typical session warm-starts
- Perf artifact: test-harness-config.json (we will attach)

Deliverables we'll share: baseline CSV of latencies, run scripts, and MAP PDF. Marissa — can you run an internal baseline with the harness and share the CSV? Ben — I'll add the MAP doc to HubSpot and flag procurement.

Thanks,
Aisha Rahman
Director, Solutions
Redwood Inference

From: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.inference.com>

Cc: Elena Ruiz <elena@stellahealth.ai>, Stella Health SRE <sre@stellahealth.ai>
Date: Mon, 02 Aug 2027 10:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: MAP request — baseline run and preliminary results

Aisha / team — I ran a dry-run on our staging cluster using the harness configured to Stella's token histogram. Notes and top-line numbers below (attached full CSV). Test run details:
- Model variant: redwood-open-13b-quant-v2 (streaming enabled)
- Hardware: 2xA100 (40GB) equivalent pool, continuous batching disabled for first-token measurement, KV cache warm for 60% of sessions
- Concurrency shape: steady 12 sessions, random jitter (±10%)

Preliminary TTFT & streaming numbers (single-run):
- TTFT p50: 68ms, p95: 198ms, p99: 410ms
- Streaming inter-token (first-2) p50: 32ms, p95: 142ms, p99: 290ms
- End-to-end (180 tokens) p50: 610ms, p95: 1060ms, p99: 1520ms

Observations / risks:
- P99 is sensitive to simultaneous KV-warm misses — recommend we run a Stella-shaped warmup (prefix reuse) in their staging run to reduce tail.
- We saw occasional CPU scheduling spikes on the orchestration node during a concurrent restart (2 events > 1s) — we'll recommend a small canary window and a dedicated orchestration instance during the test.

Attached: ttft_stream_results.csv (csv) with raw per-request timings. Next steps: schedule the customer staging run (we estimate 90–120 minutes to complete) and provide them the harness container.

- Marissa
Marissa Cole
Perf Engineer, Redwood Inference
