title: Pricing exhibit: volume proration, packaging repack & operational billing mapping
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Ingrid M. Lopez <ingrid@greenwavehealth.com>
Cc: Sanjay Rao <procurement@greenwavehealth.com>
Date: Sun, 25 Jul 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Initial: pricing exhibit questions on proration & packaging operational mapping

Hi Ingrid / Sanjay —

Thanks again for the call today. Attached is the Pricing Exhibit (v3) you shared — I took a first pass with the commercial team and we have three operational/legal items we need to clarify before we can accept the language as-is: 

1) Mid-quarter proration: the exhibit has a \"mid-month\" proration rule tied to SKU activation. Greenwave's implementation is mid-quarter for certain bundled SKUs. We need the contract language to explicitly allow quarterly proration (not strictly calendar-month) for bundled SKUs and list which SKU classes qualify. Could you confirm whether your ops team can accept a quarterly proration trigger?

2) Packaging repack / SKU substitution: the draft includes a packaging protection (caps/floors) but is silent on repack events where a customer changes SKU packaging (e.g., moving from base-tier to bundled tier). We propose a 30-day notice window and a one-time reconciliation true-up for the quarter in which the repack occurs.

3) Billing mapping: our billing/metric mapping needs a concrete fallback when the external usage metric is ambiguous (embedding vs. generation mix). We suggest an operational appendix that maps product SKUs to billing metrics and a default mapping for mixed calls (60% generation / 40% embedding -> bill at generation rate unless otherwise specified).

Attached: Greenwave_Pricing_Exhibit_v3_redline.pdf (we labeled suggested redline comments). Could you loop in Oliver from legal if needed and let us know which of these are deal-breakers for procurement?

Next steps: would value a short sync (15–20m) this week if helpful.

Thanks —
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0121

Attachments: Greenwave_Pricing_Exhibit_v3_redline.pdf (annotated), exhibit_change_log.xlsx


From: Ingrid M. Lopez <ingrid@greenwavehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sanjay Rao <procurement@greenwavehealth.com>; Oliver Klein <oliver.klein@enterprise-legal.com>
Date: Mon, 26 Jul 2027 11:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Initial: pricing exhibit questions on proration & packaging operational mapping

Ben —

Appreciate the clear summary and the annotated PDF. Quick responses inline: 

1) Quarterly proration: procurement can accept quarterly proration for bundled SKUs provided the exhibit defines the trigger dates (we prefer quarter-boundary dates). We'll ask ops to produce a list of SKU classes that will be eligible; expect that list by EOD Tue.

2) Repack/substitution: procurement is comfortable with a 30-day notice plus one-time reconciliation true-up at quarter-end — but legal wants \"repack\" defined to avoid gaming (suggested definition: material change to SKU composition >15% of unit content or change in billed metric).

3) Billing mapping fallback: we can agree to an operational appendix, but legal needs the fallback to be deterministic (not percentage-based). They prefer a hierarchy (e.g., explicit SKU mapping > embedding-dominant flag > default to generation rate).

Oliver — looping you in for the repack definition and whether we can accept Ben's proposed 30-day window without additional breakout language (caps/floors interplay).

Best,
Ingrid
Head of Procurement | Greenwave Health
ingrid@greenwavehealth.com

Quoted below — open to a quick call Wed if needed.

To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ingrid M. Lopez <ingrid@greenwavehealth.com>, Sanjay Rao <procurement@greenwavehealth.com>
Date: Tue, 27 Jul 2027 08:45:00 -0700

Laura / Karthik —

Forwarding Ingrid's reply. Key asks from Greenwave: (a) make quarterly proration explicit and list eligible SKUs, (b) add a tight definition and guardrails for \"repack/substitution,\" and (c) convert our billing fallback suggestion into deterministic hierarchy language.

I attached their header plus our annotated PDF. Procurement explicitly called out that they will not accept ambiguous percentage-based fallbacks. Oliver from their outside counsel is cc'd on the thread.

Can you please: 
- Karthik: draft a redline for the repack definition that prevents gaming but gives ops room to reconcile (30-day notice + quarter true-up).
- Laura: help with commercial language for quarterly proration window and confirm acceptable fallback hierarchy for billing mapping.

We should aim to get a proposed contract redline back to Ingrid by EOD Wed to keep the deal timeline.

Thanks —
Ben


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ingrid M. Lopez <ingrid@greenwavehealth.com>, Oliver Klein <oliver.klein@enterprise-legal.com>
Date: Wed, 28 Jul 2027 10:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Pricing exhibit questions — redline suggestions attached

Ben / Laura / Ingrid / Oliver —

Attached is a proposed redline excerpt for the Pricing Exhibit addressing: (1) Quarterly proration (clarifies that proration is performed at quarter boundaries for listed bundled SKUs), (2) Repack/Substitution (defines \"Repack Event\" as a material alteration to SKU composition >15% or a change in the billed metric, requires 30-day written notice, and allows a one-time pro rata true-up for the quarter in which the event occurs), and (3) Billing fallback hierarchy (explicit prioritization: a) explicit SKU-to-metric mapping in Appendix A, b) product-maintainer flag for embedding-dominant calls, c) default to generation rate).

A few drafting notes: 
- For caps/floors: I added language that a Repack Event does not automatically reset annual caps/floors but can trigger a reconciliatory true-up. This avoids a repack being used to reset floors mid-year.
- For proration: made \"quarterly proration\" opt-in per SKU class (we tag eligible SKUs in Appendix A).
- For dispute resolution on metric mapping: proposed a 14-day operational escalation to a joint ops working group before invoking billing credits. 

If this looks good, Laura can push commercial wording tweaks and I can fold into the exhibit PDF for return to Ingrid.

Karthik Iyer
Senior Counsel, Commercial Contracts | Redwood Inference
karthik.iyer@redwood.ai

Attachment: Redline_snippet_Repack_and_Prorate.docx (referenced)


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Ingrid M. Lopez <ingrid@greenwavehealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sanjay Rao <procurement@greenwavehealth.com>, Oliver Klein <oliver.klein@enterprise-legal.com>
Date: Wed, 28 Jul 2027 16:04:00 -0700
Subject: Final: proposed commercial language for proration + packaging

Ingrid / Sanjay / Oliver —

Thanks for the quick turnaround. Below is the commercial language we propose to add to the Pricing Exhibit (high level):

1) Quarterly Proration. \"For SKU classes listed in Appendix A, charges shall be prorated on a quarterly basis (quarters defined as Jan–Mar, Apr–Jun, Jul–Sep, Oct–Dec). Proration shall be applied at the quarter boundary following written notice of activation or deactivation.\"

2) Repack Event & True-up. \"A 'Repack Event' means a material change to SKU composition greater than 15% by unit content or any change to the billed metric. Customer shall provide at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice. For the quarter in which the Repack Event occurs, the parties will perform a one-time pro rata true-up to align invoicing with actual shipped/processed units; such true-up shall not be used to reset annual caps or floors absent mutual written agreement.\"

3) Billing Mapping Hierarchy. \"Billing mapping for ambiguous usage shall follow the hierarchy in Appendix A: (i) explicit SKU-to-metric mapping; (ii) product flag indicating embedding-dominant usage; (iii) default to generation billing rate. Disputes will escalate to the joint ops working group with a 14-day remediation window prior to billing credits.\"

If you're comfortable with this, Ben — please confirm and I'll fold into a single redline PDF and send for Oliver's review. If Oliver or Ingrid want tightening on the >15% threshold or the 14-day window, we can iterate quickly.

Thanks —
Laura Bennett
Director, Commercial Strategy | Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood.ai

---
Note: Attachments referenced in thread: Greenwave_Pricing_Exhibit_v3_redline.pdf, exhibit_change_log.xlsx, Redline_snippet_Repack_and_Prorate.docx

title: Adaptive Vector Gateway pilot — scope & kickoff (Pinecone + Contour)
preview:
From: Emma Liu <emma@pinecone.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Contour Analytics <partners@contour.ai>
Date: Wed, 21 Jul 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Pilot proposal — Adaptive Vector Gateway (pilot scope + next steps)

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the intro last week — excited to move this forward. Pinecone and Contour have sketched a compact pilot to validate an "adaptive vector gateway": a lightweight routing layer that will automatically route similarity queries to the optimal backing store (Pinecone, pgvector on S3, or a hot local cache) based on latency and cost policies.

High-level objectives:
- Demonstrate 90% traffic served from Pinecone hot indexes for high-recall queries
- Validate automatic fall-through to a pgvector cold lane stored on S3 for older documents
- Measure end-to-end 95th percentile read latency under mixed load (chat + semantic search)

Deliverables we propose for a 6-week pilot:
1) Pilot SOW and success criteria (attached)
2) Reference infra diagram and test dataset (Contour to provide sample vectors)
3) 2-week load window with joint observability (Redwood console + Pinecone metrics)

Can you confirm: (a) Redwood can provide a test tenant and API keys with a sandboxed dataset, and (b) your security/compliance team is available for a quick questionnaire run-through next week?

Attaching the draft SOW and an initial architecture diagram. Happy to adjust scope.

Best,

Emma Liu
Partnerships, Pinecone
emma@pinecone.io

text:
From: Emma Liu <emma@pinecone.io>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Contour Analytics <partners@contour.ai>
Date: Wed, 21 Jul 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Pilot proposal — Adaptive Vector Gateway (pilot scope + next steps)

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the intro last week — excited to move this forward. Pinecone and Contour have sketched a compact pilot to validate an "adaptive vector gateway": a lightweight routing layer that will automatically route similarity queries to the optimal backing store (Pinecone, pgvector on S3, or a hot local cache) based on latency and cost policies.

High-level objectives:
- Demonstrate 90% traffic served from Pinecone hot indexes for high-recall queries
- Validate automatic fall-through to a pgvector cold lane stored on S3 for older documents
- Measure end-to-end 95th percentile read latency under mixed load (chat + semantic search)

Deliverables we propose for a 6-week pilot:
1) Pilot SOW and success criteria (attached)
2) Reference infra diagram and test dataset (Contour to provide sample vectors)
3) 2-week load window with joint observability (Redwood console + Pinecone metrics)

Can you confirm: (a) Redwood can provide a test tenant and API keys with a sandboxed dataset, and (b) your security/compliance team is available for a quick questionnaire run-through next week?

Attaching the draft SOW and an initial architecture diagram. Happy to adjust scope.

Best,

Emma Liu
Partnerships, Pinecone
emma@pinecone.io
+1 (415) 555-0148

Attachments: adaptive-gateway-sow-v1.pdf, pilot-architecture-diagram.png

Quick answers to your asks:
- Test tenant: yes — we can provision a sandbox project with scoped API keys and a 30-day test dataset. Marissa, can you estimate how long to stand that up (k8s + dedicated connector to S3 + Pinecone peering)?
- Security questionnaire: Vanessa, our security folks prefer the filled checklist before we start. If Pinecone can run through a brief exchange (10–15m) next Tue or Wed that would be ideal.

A couple of redlines on the SOW:
- Add an explicit rollback plan for the gateway (we need autoscaling and a binary fallback to direct Pinecone reads if routing fails)
- Clarify ownership of vector lifecycle (who TTLs/garbage-collects cold vectors on S3)

Proposed next steps:
1) Marissa spins up sandbox and shares endpoints (ETA: 48–72h)
2) Emma/Contour complete the security checklist and share signed SOW v1
3) 15m alignment call to lock down the load window and observability hooks

If that works I can schedule the alignment call — my calendar is open Thurs afternoon.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Head of Integrations
Redwood Inference

title: Audit intake, SLA carveout & KMS/SSO handshake — next steps
preview:
From: Elena Morales <elena@acmehealth.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.example>
Cc: Tom Nguyen <tom.nguyen@acmehealth.com>
Date: 2027-07-25T08:15:00-07:00
Subject: Audit log request + temporary retention carveout

Hi Avery,

Thanks for the demo last week — good conversation. We have an incoming audit from our regulator that needs a narrow set of historical audit logs (API request traces and auth events) for a 30-day window in April. Our legal team is asking whether Redwood can provide: scope of logs, retention proofs, and whether there's any mechanism to grant a temporary retention carveout (we cannot extend retention globally).

Practical asks:
- Can you confirm what fields are in your audit export (timestamps, request ids, user ids, model ids, region tag)?
- Is there an existing short-term evidence path (ephemeral retention capture or escrow) you recommend?
- Any SLA or formal carveout process we need to open?

I attached the audit_scope_request.csv with the exact event ids the auditor expects. Can we schedule a 30-minute triage tomorrow? If you prefer, loop in your security contact.

Thanks,
Elena Morales
Head of InfoSec Compliance
Acme Health

--
attachment: audit_scope_request.csv (text/csv, 5 KB)


From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.example>
To: Elena Morales <elena@acmehealth.com>, Tom Nguyen <tom.nguyen@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.example>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.example>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.example>
Date: 2027-07-25T10:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Audit log request + temporary retention carveout

Elena — thanks for the clear ask and the CSV. We're able to support the data types you list, but there are a few coordination items we should triage so we give you a clean, auditable export rather than a manual dump.

High-level plan (proposed):
1) Quick triage call (30 min) to confirm scope and Redaction rules — Tomorrow 10:30am PT?
2) We will prepare an export template (fields + sample) and a short cover memo explaining retention policies and any required exception work.
3) If a temporary retention carveout is required, we'll propose a scoped, time-boxed escrow workflow (we've done a similar short window for healthcare customers). That requires sign-off from our Legal and Prod Security teams.

Immediate asks from our side:
- Grace: can you prepare the export field checklist and a sample export by EOD today?
- Jonas: please flag any KMS/BYOK handshake gating (if we need a customer-managed key, timeline and contact).
- Ben: flag procurement/contract language — this may need a short SLA addendum.

I attached an initial sla-draft.pdf that frames how we typically handle carveouts. We'll refine on the call.

Elena, if that works, I'll send a calendar invite for 10:30am PT tomorrow and include our Legal/security leads as optional.

Best,
Avery Johnson
Senior Account Executive, Redwood Inference
avery_johnson@redwood.example | +1 415-555-0123

--
attachment: sla-draft.pdf (application/pdf, 38 KB)

From: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.example>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.example>, Jonas Weber <jonas_weber@redwood.example>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.example>

Date: 2027-07-25T15:23:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Audit log request + temporary retention carveout

Thanks, Avery / Elena. Quick notes after scanning the CSV and initial systems check:

- Export fields: we can produce timestamp, request_id, model_id, token_count, route, requester_ip (anonymizable), and auth_principal. We do not store full raw prompt content in the default audit export (that lives in separate secured traces) — if the auditor needs prompts we need a clear legal basis.
- Retention: April window is outside default 90-day ephemeral coverage for your org. We can produce a scoped export from object storage but it will be an on-demand, manual export that requires approvals.
- KMS/BYOK: If Acme requires retrieval tied to a customer-managed key, that adds ~2–3 business days to coordinate the handshake and key retrieval process.
- Redaction: we can supply a redaction checklist; if PHI is expected we need Eliza in ProdSec on the call.

I'll prepare the export field checklist and a one-page memo (attachment: kms-handshake-notes.txt) before the call. For the triage: I can make 10:30am PT tomorrow work.

Grace O'Connor
Product Security Manager, Redwood Inference

> Elena — thanks for the clear ask and the CSV. We're able to support the data types you list...

- We will produce a scoped export for the April 30-day window as a time-boxed delivery (read-only CSV), signed-off by Legal. ETA: 4 business days from sign-off.
- If Acme requires logs tied to a customer-managed KMS key, we do a BYOK handshake. That will add ~3 business days. Acme, please confirm if you need BYOK or our default encrypted-at-rest export.

Action items:

Subject: Post-discovery alignment: next meeting options & cross-functional intros

Hi Ben —

Great to chat today, thanks for walking through Orion Health's priorities. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we keep momentum: 

Summary from today's call:
- Primary goal: lower inference costs on the batch claims pipeline while maintaining 95th pctl latency below 300ms for the UI experience.
- Constraints: must satisfy SOC 2 evidence requirements, and procurement needs a high-level cost model for FY28.
- Open questions: SSO integration timing and data residency for analytics exports.

Proposed next step: 45–60m technical deep dive + platform demo where we'll:
1) run a short end-to-end walkthrough of a representative pipeline (30m)
2) cover SSO/architecture + security evidence path (15m)
3) discuss procurement path and pricing (10–15m)

Two proposed slots (all ET):
- Tue Jul 27, 10:30–11:30 AM ET
- Wed Jul 28, 2:00–3:00 PM ET

If neither works, share 2–3 times that do and we’ll accommodate.

Can you loop in whoever owns security and procurement at Orion? If you prefer, I can bring our security lead and procurement SME (cc'd when you say who to include). 

I attached the Redwood one-pager and a short SOC2 evidence outline to give your security team a head start.

Thanks — looking forward to locking this in.

title: Elastic headroom & autoscale proposal for Sentinel Tech (Dedicated)
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
To: Jordan Peters <jordan@sentineltech.com>
Cc: Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 21 Jul 2027 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Elastic headroom & autoscale proposal for Sentinel Tech (Dedicated)

Jordan —

Thanks again for the sync this week. Per our conversation, attached is a first-pass sizing recommendation for your Dedicated deployment and an autoscale policy options matrix. High-level summary:

- Baseline slab: 6x A100-80GB (reserved) to cover steady-state inference and KV cache warm resident.
- Burst pool: 2x H100 instances reserved for short-lived spikes (up to 15 minutes) and warm pre-warm policy.
- Headroom multiplier: start at 1.4x of observed peak QPS (configurable after month-1 telemetry).
- Autoscale thresholds: scale-up at 70% used capacity sustained for 30s; scale-down after 3m idle.
- Cooldown & burst cap: bursts allowed for up to 15 minutes every hour per customer; beyond that, graceful fallback to a slightly lower-cost model variant.
- Target p90 latency for SLO: 120ms for 512-token average requests (we'll refine after your payload profile).

Immediate asks from your side:
1) Confirm expected peak concurrent requests for the first 6 months and any anticipated weekly seasonality windows.
2) Typical sequence length (median and 95th pct).
3) Procurement timeline & any approval milestones (to align pre-warm timing).

Attachments: sentinel-gpu-sizing-v1.xlsx (breaks down CPU/GPU/throughput calculations) and burst-sla-exception-request.pdf (templated language for temporary burst SLA exceptions).

If you prefer a more conservative approach, we can shift to 8x A100 baseline and remove H100 burst, but that increases committed cost ~22%.

Proposed next step: a short follow-up tomorrow to lock on peak numbers, then we'll prepare a Mutual Action Plan and a DocuSign for the Dedicated order.

Best,
Camila Reyes
Senior Solutions Architect | Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.ai
+1 415-555-0217



From: Jordan Peters <jordan@sentineltech.com>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
Cc: ops@sentineltech.com
Date: Thu, 22 Jul 2027 08:56:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Elastic headroom & autoscale proposal for Sentinel Tech (Dedicated)

Camila —

Thanks for the thorough draft. A couple quick data points to help you refine:
- Baseline steady QPS (business hours): ~1,100 requests/minute (spiky, short bursts around product releases).
- Night/weekend QPS: ~120 req/minute.
- Median token length: ~240, 95th pct: ~1,100.
- Procurement: legal sign-off expected by 2027-08-09; budget approval by 2027-07-29.

Two questions:
1) How aggressive is the pre-warm? If we have a release spike (10x traffic) that lasts 10-12 minutes, will the burst pool absorb this without impacting p90?
2) We have a procurement constraint where we can't accept open-ended SLA exceptions. Can you outline what the SLA-exception-request.pdf would need to include to go through our procurement?

Attaching our internal procurement timeline as PDF.

Appreciate the fast follow. Happy to jump on a call if easier.

—Jordan
Jordan Peters
Head of Platform Ops | Sentinel Tech
jordan@sentineltech.com


From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
To: Jordan Peters <jordan@sentineltech.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 23 Jul 2027 10:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Elastic headroom & autoscale proposal for Sentinel Tech (Dedicated)

Jordan —

Appreciate the numbers, that helps a lot. Quick answers and proposed MAP (Mutual Action Plan):

Pre-warm behavior
- The burst pool is intended to absorb 8–12x short spikes provided the requests are <15 minutes; H100 nodes are paused but kept in a warm state for faster spin-up (~45–60s cold-start versus several minutes from cold).
- For release-day 10x spikes, we recommend an aggressive pre-warm window: schedule a 10-minute pre-warm 5 minutes before the release and allow a burst cap of up to 15 minutes. That will keep p90 within target for most payload distributions.

SLA-exception language we can provide (summary):
- Timebox on burst exceptions (start/end timestamps).
- Cost and credit model for extended burst usage beyond cap.
- Commitment to model-fallback paths and degradations (e.g., revert to quantized variant with 10–15% lower quality).
- Telemetry & audit logs during burst window for post-incident review.

Mutual Action Plan (summary):
1) 2027-07-23: Redwood to deliver final sizing XLS and proposed SLA exception wording (attached).
2) 2027-07-29: Sentinel to finalize budget approval.
3) 2027-08-02: Redwood to schedule pre-warm runbook and test with a 30-minute simulated spike in staging.
4) 2027-08-09: Target legal sign-off and DocuSign execution.

DocuSign link: https://docusign.com/rc/12345-sentinel-autoscale
HubSpot deal: https://hubspot.redwood.ai/deal/ST-2027-07-21-PROD-01

I cc'd Ben and Rishi for brief technical notes on KV warm, batching and fallback variants. If that's all ok, I'll convert this MAP into a short SOW and push the DocuSign.

Thanks,
Camila
Senior Solutions Architect | Redwood Inference
camila@redwood.ai


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 23 Jul 2027 16:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Elastic headroom & autoscale proposal for Sentinel Tech (Dedicated)

Team — quick tech notes to leave in the SOW and MAP (quoted below for Camila/Jordan):

- KV cache warm: recommend a staged pre-warm: warm prefix cache on the H100 nodes 60s prior to expected spike, then maintain partial residency for 10 minutes post spike to avoid repeated cold misses. This reduces token-cost and latency; note memory necklaces on A100 vs H100 differ, so include explicit cache weights in the sizing XLS.

- Autoscale policy concrete numbers (suggested):
  * scale-up trigger: aggregate CPU+GPU util >= 70% sustained for 30s OR queue length > 250 requests for 20s.
  * scale-down: sustained < 40% for 180s.
  * burst cap: 15 minutes per hour, with a billing cap and automatic throttle to fallback-model at 18 minutes.
  * pre-warm window: 60s before scheduled events, automatic if HubSpot deal stage enters "Release Pre-warm".

- Fallback strategy: when burst cap exhausted, route new requests to quantized-8bit variant (same weights, lower memory/KV usage). Add explicit quality monitoring with an eval set for the first 24 hours after a fallback to catch regressions.

- Ops runbook: include a single-click rollback (route to hosted endpoint) and an on-call escalation path to Redwood infra lead (Rishi).

I'll add these as a paragraph in the SOW and mark the sizing sheet with the cache weight notes. Camila — want me to re-run the throughput sim with Jordan's 95th pct length and attach results?

—Ben
Senior Product Manager, Dedicated | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.ai

title: Qualification: private GPU enclave (capacity & SLA) — next milestones
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Danielle Kline <danielle.kline@quantifybio.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 22 Jul 2027 15:04:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call: private GPU enclave — recap, sizing hypothesis, and asks

Hi Danielle —

Thanks for the time today. Quick recap from our call and the concrete asks I have for your team so we can validate cost and procurement timing. I tried to keep this short and actionable.

What we heard (your stated pain):
- Model latency and throughput variability when jobs run concurrently across multi-tenant endpoints; this spikes during nightly batch runs for training-inference pipelines.
- PHI-sensitive scoring workload that must be isolated (no shared tenancy with other customers).
- Need predictable monthly spend and an option to burst for heavy ETL/refresh windows.

Quantified goals you mentioned:
- Serve 200–300 concurrent short-sequence inference requests (<=512 tokens) at p95 latency <150ms; p99 target for interactive flows 200–300ms.
- Nightly batch window that can process ~3M tokens in a 2–3 hour slot (we estimated throughput requirement below).
- Contract horizon: 12–24 months; procurement team prefers a committed monthly reservation.

Our initial technical hypothesis / proposal:
- Private GPU enclave (single-tenant pool) using reserved capacity: baseline 8x A100-equivalent GPUs (multi-instance serving) with a burst policy to +4 GPUs for scheduled batch windows.
- Autoscaling policy: scheduled scale-up for nightly batches (00:00–04:00 local) + reactive scale based on queue depth.
- Isolation: network and tenancy isolation (VPC peering + dedicated node pool), KMS-managed keys for model artifacts, and audit logging forwarded to your SIEM.
- Suggested SLA targets: 99.95% monthly uptime for the dedicated pool; p99 generation latency SLO depends on model choice (we can lock at 220ms p99 for the first 30 days, optimize via quantization and kernel tuning thereafter).

Open questions / asks from QuantifyBio to confirm before we finalize sizing:
1) Can you share a short sample of typical inference request size distribution (token counts) and arrival pattern (QPS by hour) or allow us to run a 72-hour load capture?
2) Any hard constraints on model residency (must models live in your VPC vs acceptable to host in Redwood-managed VPC with customer key control)?
3) Procurement timeline and any forms we should complete (PO, MSA redlines, SOC 2 evidence requests).

Next steps I propose (my owners in parentheses):
- Ben: deliver revised MOP + capacity spreadsheet with alternate sizing (6/8/12 GPUs) — attached. Target: EOD Friday.
- Danielle: confirm whether QuantifyBio requires on-prem model residency or VPC-hosted with KMS — by Monday.
- Marissa: schedule short architecture review with our infra team if residency is on-prem.
- Evelyn: prepare SOC 2 evidence packet and SOC 2 scope summary (attached readme).

Attachments: quantifybio-mop.xlsx (capacity scenarios), redwood-dedicated-pricing-v2.pdf, soc2-evidence-readme.txt

If this looks right I’ll synthesize a short mutual action plan (with milestones and acceptance criteria) and circulate to procurement.

Thanks again — speak soon.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0133



From: Danielle Kline <danielle.kline@quantifybio.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Arjun Patel <arjun.patel@quantifybio.com>, QuantifyBio Procurement <procurement@quantifybio.com>
Date: Thu, 22 Jul 2027 16:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: private GPU enclave — recap, sizing hypothesis, and asks

Thanks Ben — this is great, very clear. Quick answers inline: 

> 1) request size distribution / arrival pattern
We’ll export a 72-hour sampling of production traffic from our inference service — includes token counts and timestamps. Expect that by Monday morning.

> 2) model residency
For the initial phase, we prefer models to be hosted within our VPC (we have strict PHI controls). We are open to a Redwood-managed control plane, but the model artifacts and keys must live in our VPC/KMS. We’ll scope network connectivity and a short security checklist.

> 3) procurement timeline
Procurement is targeting a signed agreement by end of August. I’ve looped Arjun (cc) who manages vendor onboarding and PO issuance — Arjun, any specific docs you need?

Two quick asks back: can you highlight what observability we’ll get per-model (token-level cost, latency heatmaps, KV cache hit rates)? Also we need example runbooks for failover if the enclave becomes unavailable during batch windows.

I’ll review the attachments shortly.

—Danielle
Director of ML Platforms | QuantifyBio



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Danielle Kline <danielle.kline@quantifybio.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 23 Jul 2027 08:47:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: private GPU enclave — recap, sizing hypothesis, and asks

Perfect — appreciate the quick clarifications and the VPC constraint, that helps a lot. Two fast replies and an updated plan: 

Observability & runbooks:
- Per-model dashboards: token-level cost, per-route latency distribution (p50/p95/p99), concurrency heatmaps, KV cache hit/miss rates — standard in Redwood Console for Dedicated pools.
- We’ll share an example failover playbook: automatic fallback to a secondary (Redwood-managed) pool with model variants pinned, plus a graceful queue drain policy. That will be in the MOP attachment.

Updated sizing note (attachment updated):
- Given the PHI and VPC constraint, our recommendation is a baseline 10x A100-equivalent reserved GPUs in your enclave with scheduled +4 GPUs for batch windows. The extra baseline buffer reduces p99 tails and gives headroom for model experiments.
- If procurement prefers lower committed spend, we can offer a 6-GPU baseline + burst to 10 for the same contract term; I included both scenarios in quantifybio-mop.xlsx.

Immediate next steps I’ll take now:
1) Finalize the mutual action plan (MOP) with milestones and acceptance criteria — I’ll send a version by EOD today.
2) Marissa to coordinate architecture call if you confirm on-prem/VPC residency — propose Tue 27 Jul 10:00AM PT.
3) Evelyn will package the SOC 2 snapshot and an initial list of evidence items for procurement.

Danielle — can Arjun confirm the procurement docs he needs (MSA redlines, vendor security questionnaire, PO format)? That way we can route the right attachments to procurement in one pass.

Thanks — attaching updated pricing + MOP snapshot.

Best,
Ben



From: Arjun Patel <arjun.patel@quantifybio.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Danielle Kline <danielle.kline@quantifybio.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>, QuantifyBio Procurement <procurement@quantifybio.com>
Date: Fri, 23 Jul 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: private GPU enclave — qualification: procurement checklist

Ben / Danielle —

From procurement side, helpful items: 
- Draft MSA (redlines accepted in tracked changes) or our standard PO template (we can accept PO only if the MSA is in place).
- SOC 2 Type II report or attestation + summary of controls and data residency statement.
- W-9 and vendor onboarding form (I’ll attach our standard forms).
- Pricing breakdown by committed tier and burst usage (line items) — PDF or spreadsheet.

If you can drop a single ZIP with the required artifacts (MSA draft, SOC 2 readme, pricing spreadsheet, W-9) that would speed review. Danielle / Arjun, procurement is comfortable reviewing an initial draft MSA and then routing through legal.

Thanks — Arjun
Vendor Ops | QuantifyBio

title: Rapid privacy engagement: notification timelines & subprocessor access request
preview:
From: Alice White <alice.white@finnova.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: security@finnova.com
Date: 2027-07-22T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Clarify incident notice window + subprocessor access (DPA section 5/7)

Ben —

Thanks again for taking this call this morning. As we prepare for procurement, the legal team flagged two things in the DPA we need clarity on before we can sign:

1) Notification timeline: Section 7 says \"promptly and without undue delay\" but Finnova needs a contractual timeline we can rely on to meet our regulator reporting obligations (we're required to notify within 72 hours of becoming aware). Can Redwood commit to an initial customer notification within 48 or 72 hours of confirmed unauthorized access? Also, how do you distinguish \"aware\" vs \"confirmed\"?

2) Subprocessor cooperation: If a Redwood subprocessor holds logs or network captures related to a customer-impacting incident, we need a defined evidence hand-off procedure that:
   - preserves chain of custody,
   - gives us access to redacted session logs within a reasonable window, and
   - specifies who pays for forensic work if third-party engagement is required.

Please see attached a redline markup from our counsel with preferred language (we didn't edit the full DPA yet). Can you send a short proposal for both the notification window and the evidence/subprocessor access flow so I can share it internally?

Appreciate the urgency — Finnova is prioritizing this as part of their FedRAMP-ish checklist.

— Alice
Alice White
Head of Security and Privacy
Finnova Health

Attachments: Finnova_DPA_comments_v3.pdf (attached by Finnova)


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Alice White <alice.white@finnova.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-07-22T10:14:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Clarify incident notice window + subprocessor access (DPA section 5/7)

Alice —

Thanks for the clear ask and the redline. Looping in Marissa (Legal) and Lauren (Ops) so we can get a coordinated response and proposed clause quickly. High level from my side:

- We generally aim to notify impacted customers within 72 hours of confirmed unauthorized access; we do not want to commit to a 48-hour timeline broadly because some incidents require initial triage to avoid false positives.
- \"Aware\" vs \"confirmed\": our internal definition is (a) \"reasonable suspicion\" when indicators show unauthorized access and (b) \"confirmed\" when forensics validate unauthorized access to customer data. We can include both concepts in the DPA so that we (i) send an initial notification upon suspicion and (ii) follow up with a confirmation within the fixed window.
- For subprocessor-held evidence, we can commit to a documented hand-off process: preserved artifacts within 10 business days for direct download (secure SFTP), redacted logs on request within 5 business days, and a point-of-contact in ops for escalations. Forensic third-party costs are typically borne by the customer only if the incident is caused by customer misconfiguration; otherwise Redwood covers basic evidence exports and coordination.

Next steps: Marissa will propose contract language and Lauren will outline runbook SLAs for evidence access. I'll circle back with a short proposed clause by early afternoon.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive — Enterprise
Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
To: Alice White <alice.white@finnova.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-07-22T12:03:00-07:00
Subject: Proposed DPA language and evidence handback protocol

Alice / Finnova team —

Following Ben's note and our internal discussions, below is proposed contractual text we can add to the DPA. This is written to balance regulator timing needs and operational realities for multi-tenant infra. Please review and let me know required edits.

Proposed clause (draft):
\"Incident Notice and Cooperation.
1) Initial notice: Redwood will provide Customer with an initial incident notification within 24 hours of Redwood's reasonable suspicion of unauthorized access affecting Customer systems or data. The initial notice will include summary indicators and an assigned incident reference.
2) Confirmation notice: Redwood will provide a confirmed incident notification (including scope and impacted data classes) within 72 hours of confirmation of unauthorized access. If Redwood cannot confirm scope within 72 hours, Redwood will provide interim updates at least every 72 hours until scope is confirmed.
3) Evidence handback: Forensic artifacts (logs, captures, and relevant metadata) maintained by Redwood or a Redwood subprocessor will be preserved upon detection and made available to Customer within 10 business days via secure transfer (SFTP) or controlled export. Redwood will provide redacted logs within 5 business days on request, subject to applicable laws and subprocessor contractual obligations.
4) Subprocessor cooperation: Redwood will engage subprocessors to obtain and transfer evidence where necessary and will use commercially reasonable efforts to secure subprocessor cooperation. Redwood will act as liaison; where a subprocessor refuses access due to legal restrictions, Redwood will provide written notice explaining the limitation and a summary of the withheld materials.
5) Costs: Redwood will bear the reasonable costs of evidence export and initial analysis unless Customer requests engagement of third-party forensic vendors or the incident results from Customer breach or misconfiguration, in which case Customer will bear those costs.
6) Escalation: Redwood will provide an escalation contact (email + mobile) for customers with active incidents; standard contact: security-incident@redwood.com (primary) and Karthik Iyer (Forensics Lead) for technical handoffs.

Operational annex: Lauren will supply an \"Evidence handback template\" and a runbook excerpt describing SFTP endpoints, PGP keys, and an anonymization checklist.

If this captures Finnova's needs we can prepare a DPA amendment and push to DocuSign (I can also surface a short playbook to help your SOC coordinate exercises).

Marissa Cole
Sr. Counsel, Privacy & Security
Redwood Inference

Attachments: Redwood_DPA_proposed_clause_v7.pdf; evidence_handback_template.xlsx


From: Alice White <alice.white@finnova.com>

Date: 2027-07-22T13:20:00-07:00

Marissa —

Thank you — this is very helpful and closer to what our counsel wants. A few clarifying asks before we sign:

- Can \"initial notice\" be defined as \"within 24 hours of reasonable suspicion\" but with the confirmation timeline explicitly \"within 72 hours of Redwood confirmation\" as you wrote? Finnova's regulators want an auditable timestamp for when Redwood first informed us (so our SOC can show we engaged within the statutory window).
- Regarding redacted logs: please confirm whether redaction will remove customer-identifying fields but preserve sequence/timing so our forensics can reconstruct timelines.
- If a subprocessor operates in a jurisdiction that bars export of certain logs, will Redwood provide a detailed written summary of the withheld material and an explanation of the legal basis within the same 10 business day window?
- Finally — can you confirm the escalation contact phone number for Karthik? We run tabletop tests quarterly and would like to schedule a dry run once the DPA amendment is executed.

If you can confirm these points I can take the updated clause to counsel for final sign-off.

Alice

Hi Lily,

Thanks again for the deep dive yesterday. Attached is a compact add-on proposal that lines up with the use cases you described: 1) moderate live trace retention for key services, 2) sampled tracing for high-volume streams, and 3) an advanced dashboard pack for observability teams and SREs. I included two packaging options in the PDF and a simple cost model spreadsheet for your finance team.

High level summary:
- Option A: Seat-based dashboard + tiered retention buckets (recommended pilot)
- Option B: Metered retention + capped advanced-dashboard seats (alternative for bursty usage)

Ben,

Thanks for the doc and model. A few quick clarifying questions from our side:

1) Billing cadence: we'd prefer monthly invoicing initially while we're in pilot. Annual is possible after we validate savings.
2) Sampling: can you outline how sampled tracing affects alert fidelity? We need to ensure SLO alerts don't degrade.
3) Dashboards: how do you handle concurrent seat limits vs read-only viewers? We have a mix of SREs and product owners who only need view access.
4) Any downside to Option B long-term if our traffic spikes during a campaign?

Also looping in our ops alias for procurement notes.

Thanks,
Lily
Director, Observability
Orbit Analytics
lily@orbitanalytics.com

---



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Lily Tran <lily@orbitanalytics.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 21 Jul 2027 14:26:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Scoped observability: retention tapering & advanced dashboard licensing for Orbit

Lily,

Great questions — answers below and a short tradeoff summary so you can share with the SREs and finance folks.

1) Billing: Monthly pilot invoicing is fine. We typically apply a conversion credit if you convert to annual within 90 days. I put two conversion scenarios in the spreadsheet (5% and 12% credit).

2) Sampling and alert fidelity: sampled tracing keeps 100% of traces for error rates and throttles non-error traces according to the sampling policy. For SLOs tied to error budgets or tail latency, we can keep a higher sample rate on high-priority endpoints. The practical tradeoff is raw per-token cost vs. diagnostic fidelity. For the pilot we propose a 20% global sample with 100% capture of error traces; that preserves alerting while saving ingest costs.

3) Dashboards seats vs viewers: We offer two seat types: editor seats (full query/run) and viewer seats (read-only). Viewer seats are much cheaper; for your org we can map product owners to viewer seats and SREs to editors. The advanced-dashboard pack includes up to 6 editor seats by default; additional viewer seats are inexpensive.

4) Option B spike risk: Option B is meter-first; it performs well in steady-state and gives flexibility during bursts, but if you see sustained spikes it can become more expensive than a slab/seat commitment. We can put a burst cap and automatic fallback to a pooled reserved capacity in the Dedicated layer if you want predictability during campaigns.

Proposed concession for pilot
- 6-week pilot, monthly billing, 50% credit on observability add-on fees for pilot period
- 2-week technical onboarding and trace-sampling tuning with an embedded engineer
- Clear exit criteria: 10% improvement in mean-time-to-detect or equivalent ops effort reduction; plus tolerance thresholds for cost delta

If that looks good I can feed this into a one-page SOW and routing to Procurement. Kimberly and Rachel, can you confirm legal/sales terms we should include?

Thanks,
Ben

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Lily Tran <lily@orbitanalytics.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>, Rachel Kim <rachel@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 21 Jul 2027 14:26:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Scoped observability: retention tapering & advanced dashboard licensing for Orbit

Lily,

Great questions — answers below and a short tradeoff summary so you can share with the SREs and finance folks.

1) Billing: Monthly pilot invoicing is fine. We typically apply a conversion credit if you convert to annual within 90 days. I put two conversion scenarios in the spreadsheet (5% and 12% credit).

2) Sampling and alert fidelity: sampled tracing keeps 100% of traces for error rates and throttles non-error traces according to the sampling policy. For SLOs tied to error budgets or tail latency, we can keep a higher sample rate on high-priority endpoints. The practical tradeoff is raw per-token cost vs. diagnostic fidelity. For the pilot we propose a 20% global sample with 100% capture of error traces; that preserves alerting while saving ingest costs.

3) Dashboards seats vs viewers: We offer two seat types: editor seats (full query/run) and viewer seats (read-only). Viewer seats are much cheaper; for your org we can map product owners to viewer seats and SREs to editors. The advanced-dashboard pack includes up to 6 editor seats by default; additional viewer seats are inexpensive.

4) Option B spike risk: Option B is meter-first; it performs well in steady-state and gives flexibility during bursts, but if you see sustained spikes it can become more expensive than a slab/seat commitment. We can put a burst cap and automatic fallback to a pooled reserved capacity in the Dedicated layer if you want predictability during campaigns.

Proposed concession for pilot
- 6-week pilot, monthly billing, 50% credit on observability add-on fees for pilot period
- 2-week technical onboarding and trace-sampling tuning with an embedded engineer
- Clear exit criteria: 10% improvement in mean-time-to-detect or equivalent ops effort reduction; plus tolerance thresholds for cost delta

If that looks good I can feed this into a one-page SOW and routing to Procurement. Kimberly and Rachel, can you confirm legal/sales terms we should include?

Thanks,
Ben

title: Visibility bridge: on-prem telemetry export and SIEM mapping
preview:
From: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@acmehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: splunk-integration@acmehealth.com
Date: Mon, 19 Jul 2027 09:03:12 -0400
Subject: Visibility bridge: on-prem SIEM export question

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the walk-through this morning. We're finalizing our telemetry export requirements and I'd like to confirm a few items so we can move the security review forward: 

- Our compliance team needs an auditable delivery path for logs and traces from the on-prem enclave to our Splunk tenant (HEC) without opening inbound ports into the enclave.
- We need token rotation guidance and an evidence bundle for SOC2 auditors (audit stamps, retention proof).
- Can Redwood support OTLP -> Splunk HEC directly, or do we need a managed forwarder inside our perimeter?

I've attached a short spreadsheet that lists the collectors we currently run (acme_onprem_requirements.xlsx). Can your team propose 2-3 supported patterns (preferred, fallback, third-party-forwarder) and a short risk table for each?

Appreciate it — want to get this to legal + security by EOD Wednesday.

Thanks,
Tom Ellis
Senior Security Engineer, Acme Health
Tom.Ellis@acmehealth.com

Attachments: acme_onprem_requirements.xlsx (spreadsheet, 48KB)


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Tom Ellis <tom.ellis@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.ai>, Lauren Bishop <lauren.bishop@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 19 Jul 2027 11:02:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Visibility bridge: on-prem SIEM export question

Tom —

Thanks for the clear ask and for the spreadsheet. High level we can support multiple integration patterns; I'll summarize three recommended patterns below and then propose a quick architecture doc and an on-site/remote workshop to finalize.

1) Managed forwarder (recommended)
   - Deploy a lightweight Redwood forwarder (TLS client) inside your enclave that pushes to Splunk HEC over TLS (no inbound).
   - Benefits: simple, low-latency for alerts, direct mapping to fields, easier audit logs on push attempts.
   - Tradeoffs: requires a small footprint inside your perimeter and responsibility for certificate management.

2) Pull/proxy model (auditable storage bridge)
   - Collector writes encrypted daily batches to a customer-controlled S3/GCS bucket in the VPC; a hardened puller in a DMZ pulls and forwards to HEC.
   - Benefits: strong custody chain and retention proof, easy to snapshot for audits.
   - Tradeoffs: higher latency, more ops coordination.

3) OTLP -> OTLP collector with transformer (no direct HEC)
   - Use an OTLP-compatible collector inside the enclave to send traces to an internal OTEL gateway; Redwood or a partner transformer converts to HEC with mutual-TLS.
   - Benefits: preserves trace fidelity, central schema enforcement, minimal change to instrumented apps.
   - Tradeoffs: more components, needs handshake for cert pinning.

Security controls we recommend for any option: token rotation cadence (90 days), mutual-TLS with certificate pinning for HEC endpoints, idempotent delivery with sequence numbers, and signed audit receipts for each push. I'll attach a sample HEC config and a short risk/mitigation table.

Would you be available for a 60-minute workshop Wednesday 2027-07-21 10:00-11:00 ET? We'll bring a diagram and an evidence checklist.

Regards,
Ben Carter
Strategic Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0123

Attachments: redwood_hec_example.conf (example HEC client snippet)


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Soojin Lee <soojin.lee@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 20 Jul 2027 09:15:10 -04:00
Subject: Re: Visibility bridge: on-prem SIEM export question

Ben / team —

Following up after a quick internal sync. I sketched three candidate designs and a short risk matrix; attaching as PDF (visibility_options_v1.pdf). Key engineering notes:

- For traces we should prefer OTLP inside the enclave and a purpose-built transformer to HEC; this avoids loss of span attributes when mapping directly to HEC events.
- For logs, our forwarder supports syslog over TLS, HEC, and S3 batch delivery. We should standardize on field mappings (timestamp, service.name, env, request_id) and produce a one-page mapping table for their Splunk team.
- Evidence for SOC2: we can produce signed push receipts and a weekly delivery manifest with checksums. That will meet retention and integrity requirements.

Open questions for Karthik / Soojin:
- Do we want to propose certificate pinning + mutual-TLS as standard (adds friction but very strong protection)?
- How much of the forwarder footprint can be packaged as an operator for their Kubernetes clusters vs an appliance for legacy hosts?

I'll be on the call Wednesday and can walk through the PDF.

Marissa Cole
Product Manager, Observability Integrations | Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.ai

Attachment: visibility_options_v1.pdf (diagram + risk matrix)

On Mon, Jul 19, 2027 at 11:02 AM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Tom —
> ...

Cc: splunk-integration@acmehealth.com
Date: Wed, 21 Jul 2027 14:30:00 -04:00

This is great, thank you. We're aligned on option (1) as the primary recommendation (managed forwarder) and option (2) as a fallback for high-sensitivity zones. The security team is firm on mutual-TLS and certificate pinning for the forwarder.

Next steps:
- Please send the one-page Splunk field mapping and an installer/manifest for the forwarder by EOD Thursday (2027-07-22).
- We'll run the proposed certificate rotation process through our PKI team and get back with any constraints.

title: Proposed escrow schedule and invoice routing for Order Form
preview:
From: Tom Ruiz <tom.ruiz@atlasdata.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Sood <procurement@atlasdata.com>
Date: 2027-07-12T09:18:00-07:00
Subject: Order Form: escrow schedule and invoicing path question

Hi Ben,

Quick follow on the Order Form draft you sent last week (thanks). Procurement at Atlas raised two practical questions we need to resolve before countersigning:

1) Escrow / holdback: our legal team prefers the funded-escrow style for the first milestone (20%) until production go-live. Is Redwood open to a 60-day holdback that is released on go-live? If so, how would you prefer we reflect that in the invoice(s)?

2) VAT / tax treatment: Atlas operates across US and EU entities. On the pricing schedule we need clarity on whether Redwood will invoice gross (including VAT) or net of VAT and whether Atlas should self-assess. We can share our tax residence docs but need Redwood to confirm what it requires to avoid withholding.

Practical asks: can you confirm (a) whether Redwood would accept one invoice with a line-item for the holdback vs two separate invoices, and (b) whether Redwood needs entity-level tax evidence attached at signing or at first invoice?

We'd like to close this week if possible.

Thanks,
Tom
Tom Ruiz
Head of Procurement, AtlasData
procurement@atlasdata.com

---
Attachment: procurement_requirements.pdf (stub)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Tom Ruiz <tom.ruiz@atlasdata.com>
Cc: Priya Sood <procurement@atlasdata.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-07-13T10:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Order Form: escrow schedule and invoicing path question

Tom — thanks for laying this out. Short answers up front: we can support a holdback (we typically call it a \"contractual holdback\" rather than escrow) and we prefer a single invoice with a holdback line-item for bookkeeping simplicity. On VAT: we need entity-level tax residence evidence before issuing the first invoice net of VAT.

Details / proposal:
- Invoice sequencing: issue a single invoice for the milestone amount with a separate line for \"Holdback (20%)\" which will be retained and released per the milestone acceptance criteria. Our AR team prefers the single-invoice approach so reconciliation is easier on both sides.
- Timing: Redwood will release holdback within 60 days of Production Go-Live confirmation (we can include acceptance checklist in the Order Form).
- Tax evidence: please upload the TaxResidenceMatrix.xlsx and scanned W-8/W-9 or EU tax certs. We will need this prior to issuing net-of-VAT invoices. If you cannot provide, Redwood will invoice gross and Atlas can claim per local rules.

Next steps: I've looped in Laura (legal) and Evelyn (finance) to propose specific language and a redline. I've attached our suggested redline (OrderForm_v3_redlines.pdf) and a sample tax matrix for you to fill out (TaxResidenceMatrix.xlsx). Can Atlas confirm by EOD Friday?

Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales — Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0182

Attachments: OrderForm_v3_redlines.pdf, TaxResidenceMatrix.xlsx

From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
To: Tom Ruiz <tom.ruiz@atlasdata.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Sood <procurement@atlasdata.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2027-07-14T08:42:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Order Form: escrow schedule and invoicing path question

Tom / Ben — attaching a redline with two small legal clarifications. Summary:

- Replace the word \"escrow\" with \"contractual holdback\" in the Order Form and add a schedule that ties release to the Production Acceptance Checklist. (Escrow agents introduce operational complexity; a contractually permitted holdback keeps collections and releases under Redwood control.)
- Net terms: propose language under Section 3 that Redwood will invoice net of VAT where Atlas provides valid tax evidence per-entity prior to the first invoice; otherwise Redwood invoices gross.
- Invoicing format: accept single invoice with holdback line-item. Add a required PO number field on the first invoice for proper matching; without PO we cannot process net-of-VAT.

Suggested contract snippet (for insertion into Order Form):
\"Payment and Holdback: Customer will be invoiced for the milestone amount with a holdback line equal to 20% of the milestone. Redwood will retain the holdback and release it within sixty (60) days of Customer providing Production Acceptance Confirmation in accordance with Schedule A.\"

I've also added a clause allowing an accounting credit if Atlas needs the holdback to be treated as a separate invoice for internal controls — this preserves flexibility without changing Redwood's preferred workflow.

Please review the attached PDF redline. If Atlas is comfortable with the single-invoice approach we can prepare the DocuSign bundle and Evelyn will confirm final invoicing instructions.

Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Contracts — Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood.com

Hi Ben,

Quick follow on the Order Form draft you sent last week (thanks). Procurement at Atlas raised two practical questions we need to resolve before countersigning:

1) Escrow / holdback: our legal team prefers the funded-escrow style for the first milestone (20%) until production go-live. Is Redwood open to a 60-day holdback that is released on go-live? If so, how would you prefer we reflect that in the invoice(s)?

2) VAT / tax treatment: Atlas operates across US and EU entities. On the pricing schedule we need clarity on whether Redwood will invoice gross (including VAT) or net of VAT and whether Atlas should self-assess. We can share our tax residence docs but need Redwood to confirm what it requires to avoid withholding.

Practical asks: can you confirm (a) whether Redwood would accept one invoice with a line-item for the holdback vs two separate invoices, and (b) whether Redwood needs entity-level tax evidence attached at signing or at first invoice?

We'd like to close this week if possible.

Thanks,
Tom

Subject: Re: Order Form: escrow schedule and invoicing path question

Tom — thanks for laying this out. Short answers up front: we can support a holdback (we typically call it a \"contractual holdback\" rather than escrow) and we prefer a single invoice with a holdback line-item for bookkeeping simplicity. On VAT: we need entity-level tax residence evidence before issuing the first invoice net of VAT.

Details / proposal:
- Invoice sequencing: issue a single invoice for the milestone amount with a separate line for \"Holdback (20%)\" which will be retained and released per the milestone acceptance criteria. Our AR team prefers the single-invoice approach so reconciliation is easier on both sides.
- Timing: Redwood will release holdback within 60 days of Production Go-Live confirmation (we can include acceptance checklist in the Order Form).
- Tax evidence: please upload the TaxResidenceMatrix.xlsx and scanned W-8/W-9 or EU tax certs. We will need this prior to issuing net-of-VAT invoices. If you cannot provide, Redwood will invoice gross and Atlas can claim per local rules.

Next steps: I've looped in Laura (legal) and Evelyn (finance) to propose specific language and a redline. I've attached our suggested redline (OrderForm_v3_redlines.pdf) and a sample tax matrix for you to fill out (TaxResidenceMatrix.xlsx). Can Atlas confirm by EOD Friday?

Ben

Tom / Ben — attaching a redline with two small legal clarifications. Summary:

- Replace the word \"escrow\" with \"contractual holdback\" in the Order Form and add a schedule that ties release to the Production Acceptance Checklist. (Escrow agents introduce operational complexity; a contractually permitted holdback keeps collections and releases under Redwood control.)
- Net terms: propose language under Section 3 that Redwood will invoice net of VAT where Atlas provides valid tax evidence per-entity prior to the first invoice; otherwise Redwood invoices gross.
- Invoicing format: accept single invoice with holdback line-item. Add a required PO number field on the first invoice for proper matching; without PO we cannot process net-of-VAT.

Suggested contract snippet (for insertion into Order Form):
\"Payment and Holdback: Customer will be invoiced for the milestone amount with a holdback line equal to 20% of the milestone. Redwood will retain the holdback and release it within sixty (60) days of Customer providing Production Acceptance Confirmation in accordance with Schedule A.\"

I've also added a clause allowing an accounting credit if Atlas needs the holdback to be treated as a separate invoice for internal controls — this preserves flexibility without changing Redwood's preferred workflow.

Please review the attached PDF redline. If Atlas is comfortable with the single-invoice approach we can prepare the DocuSign bundle and Evelyn will confirm final invoicing instructions.

Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Contracts — Redwood Inference
